patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes a regression in perf record's Zstandard compression feature, which caused it to abort recording and produce an undecompressable file due to a 'Bad address' error. The patches also fix two latent bugs: an output buffer overrun and a broken error fallback in the zstd compressor. The changes ensure that multiple compressed records are handled correctly and that raw data is not stored when compression fails.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

version_history.subject: [PATCH 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the perf record tool's ZSTD compression handling. When ZSTD compression fails, it previously attempted to copy the uncompressed data into the output buffer, which can lead to issues such as incorrect header sizes and unbounded copying. Instead, this patch propagates the error by returning -1 when compression fails, preventing these problems.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

version_history.subject: [PATCH 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the zstd compression API used by perf tools, where the output buffer could be overrun when compressing data. The issue occurred because the function did not check if there was enough space left in the output buffer to hold a full record before writing one. The patch adds a check to prevent this overwriting and returns an error instead.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

version_history.subject: [PATCH 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in perf record where multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records are emitted per push when Zstd compression is enabled and the compressed output exceeds the maximum record size. The fix involves changing how the header size is calculated to prevent underflow and truncation, allowing for correct decompression of the data. The patch also updates the test case to reflect this change.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] nfsd: recall deleg if a requested dir attr change can't be encoded

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] nfsd: recall deleg if a requested dir attr change can't be encoded

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFSv4 server where it silently drops directory attribute changes when they cannot be encoded, leading to stale cached directory attributes on the client side. The patch recalls the delegation when such an error occurs, as required by RFC 8881. It also distinguishes between genuine encode failures and benign cases where no specific dir attributes are requested.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the patch does not fully address the issue of silently masking genuine encode failures and vfs_getattr failures as 0-length attributes, which causes directory attribute change events to be omitted instead of triggering a delegation recall.
sentiment_signals: patch is not yet a complete fix
review_comments.raw_body:
Sashiko reports (and gpt-5.5 agrees):

Pre-existing issues:
- [High] Genuine encode failures and `vfs_getattr` failures are silently masked as 0-length attributes, causing the directory attribute change event to be erroneously omitted instead of triggering a delegation recall.

But this isn’t really a pre-existing issue. It pre-exists this fix, yes,
but it was introduced by “nfsd: add support to CB_NOTIFY for dir
attribute changes”. What that means is this patch is not yet a complete
fix.


-- 
Chuck Lever

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 00/26] nfs4.1: add some directory delegation testcases

version_history.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 00/26] nfs4.1: add some directory delegation testcases

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 01/26] nfs4.1: add proposed NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND flag
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new flag, NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND, to the NFSv4 protocol. The flag is proposed in RFC8881bis and allows for extensions to original directory delegations specified in RFC8881. In practice, this means that if a client requests anything other than a recall-only delegation from an NFS server running Linux, it must support this new flag.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 02/26] nfs4.1: add a getfh() to the end of create_obj() compound
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a GETFH operation to the end of the create_obj() compound in the NFSv4.1 server, allowing tests to retrieve the filehandle of newly created objects.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 03/26] server41tests: add a basic GET_DIR_DELEGATION test
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a basic test for directory delegation in the NFSv4 server, specifically testing the handout, recall, and return of directory delegations. The test is implemented using the existing server41tests framework and involves creating a session, opening a file, and then performing operations related to directory delegation. The patch includes new code for decoding notify events and converting bitmap4 values to integers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 04/26] server41tests: add a test for duplicate GET_DIR_DELEGATION requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that an NFSv4.1 server returns GDD4_UNAVAIL when it receives duplicate GET_DIR_DELEGATION requests. The test simulates sending a dir delegation request and then immediately retrying the same request, checking that the server responds with the expected status code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 05/26] server41tests: pass_warn() when server doesn't support dir delegations
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the server41tests to pass with a warning instead of failing when the server does not support directory delegations or when it fails to hand out a delegation. It also adds checks for non-fatal status codes and ensures that the correct notifications are offered by the server. The changes make it safe to keep the directory delegation tests in the 'all' group.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 06/26] server41tests: test remove triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test case to the server41tests suite, which verifies that removing a file from one client triggers a delegation recall on another client. The test creates a directory delegation without notification, opens a file in it, and then removes the file from a second client. It checks that the server issues a CB_RECALL in response.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 07/26] server41tests: test rename triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite in the pynfs project. The test verifies that when a file is renamed from one client while another client holds a directory delegation for the parent directory, the server issues a CB_RECALL operation to recall the delegation. This ensures that the server properly handles delegation recalls triggered by rename operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 08/26] server41tests: test mkdir triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that creating a subdirectory from a second client triggers a dir delegation recall on the server. The test first gets a directory delegation with no notification mask and then creates a subdirectory from a different client. It checks that the server issues a CB_RECALL in response.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 09/26] server41tests: test link triggers dir delegation recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that a directory delegation recall is triggered when a file is hard-linked from another client. The test creates a directory delegation without notification mask, creates a file, and then links it to a new name from a second client. It checks that the server issues a CB_RECALL in response. This patch aims to ensure that the NFSv4.1 protocol correctly handles directory delegation recalls when files are linked across clients.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 10/26] server41tests: test no notifications without GFLAG_EXTEND
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test case to the server41tests suite to verify that when a file is created in a delegated directory, the server issues a CB_RECALL instead of a CB_NOTIFY if the NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND flag is not set. The test case requests a dir delegation with NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY but without NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND and checks for the correct callback response.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 11/26] server41tests: test unrequested notification type triggers recall
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test case to the server41tests suite to verify that when an unrequested notification type is triggered, the server issues a CB_RECALL instead of a CB_NOTIFY. The test creates a directory delegation with only REMOVE notifications and then triggers an ADD event from a second client. It checks that the server responds with a CB_RECALL as expected.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 12/26] server41tests: add a test for removal from dir with dir delegation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite, specifically testing removal of a file from a directory while dir delegation is in effect. The test requests a dir delegation with REMOVE_ENTRY notifications and verifies that the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with the correct REMOVE event. Additionally, the patch updates the GET_DIR_DELEGATION request to include full child and directory attribute bitmaps for all CB_NOTIFY tests.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 13/26] server41tests: add a test for directory add notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test to the server41tests suite, specifically designed to verify that the NFSv4.1 server sends correct directory delegation notifications when a file is added to a delegated directory. The test involves requesting a dir delegation with ADD_ENTRY notifications, creating a file from a second client, and verifying that the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with the correct ADD event details.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 14/26] server41tests: add test for RENAME event notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite for RENAME event notifications. The test requests a directory delegation and then renames a file from a second client, verifying that the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with the correct RENAME event. The test checks for the presence of the notification and its contents to ensure correctness.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 15/26] server41tests: verify child attributes in ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to verify that child attributes are included in the ADD notification sent by the server when a new file is created. The test creates a directory delegation, opens a file from a second client, and then verifies that the ADD notification includes the correct size attribute for the newly created file.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 16/26] server41tests: test CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that the NFSv4.1 server sends a CHANGE_DIR_ATTRS notification when directory attributes are changed. The test creates a delegation and requests notifications for changes to directory attributes and file additions. It then creates a file from a second client and checks that the server sends the expected notification with the updated directory attributes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 17/26] server41tests: test mkdir triggers ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that when a client creates a subdirectory from another client, the server sends a CB_NOTIFY with an ADD event notification. The test requests a dir delegation with ADD_ENTRY notifications and checks that the correct directory name is included in the notification.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 18/26] server41tests: test DELEGRETURN stops notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite, specifically designed to verify that notifications are stopped when a delegation is returned using the DELEGRETURN operation. The test creates a directory delegation with ADD_ENTRY notifications enabled, immediately returns it, and then creates a file from a second client. It verifies that no callbacks are received after the delegation was returned, indicating that the notifications were successfully stopped.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 19/26] server41tests: verify filehandle in ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that the filehandle attribute is included and matches in ADD notifications. The test creates a directory delegation, opens a file from another client, records its filehandle via GETFH, and then verifies that the ADD notification includes a filehandle attribute matching the one obtained from GETFH.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 20/26] server41tests: test cross-directory rename REMOVE notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that a cross-directory rename generates a NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY notification on the source directory's delegation, rather than a NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY. The test creates a file in a delegated directory, renames it to a target directory, and checks for the correct notifications. This ensures compliance with RFC 8881bis Section 27.4.6.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 21/26] server41tests: test cross-directory rename ADD notification on target
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test to the server41tests suite, which verifies that a cross-directory rename operation generates an ADD notification on the target directory's delegation. The test creates a source directory and file, opens a delegation on the target directory, renames the file into the delegated target directory, and checks for the correct notifications. This patch is part of the pynfs v3 series and aims to ensure that the NFSv4.1 server correctly handles cross-directory rename operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 22/26] server41tests: test link triggers ADD notification
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that creating a hard link triggers an ADD notification on the directory's delegation. The test creates a file, then links it to a new name from another session. It checks that the server sends an ADD notification with the correct entry name in response to the link operation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 23/26] server41tests: test same-client changes don't trigger notifications
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that changes made by a client holding a delegation do not trigger CB_NOTIFY or CB_RECALL callbacks. The test creates a file from the delegation-holding session itself and checks that no notifications are received within a short time frame.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 24/26] server41tests: test cross-directory rename-over nad_old_entry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite to verify that when a cross-directory rename overwrites an existing file in a delegated directory, the server reports the overwritten entry via nad_old_entry in the NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY notification. The patch also fixes _getDirDeleg to initialize cb.changes as a list and use extend() to accumulate notifications, allowing tests to receive multiple CB_NOTIFY callbacks and see all notifications.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 25/26] server41tests: test within-directory rename-over nad_old_entry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the server41tests suite in the Linux NFSv4.1 implementation. The test, called 'testDirDelegRenameOver', verifies that when a within-directory rename operation overwrites an existing entry, the server correctly populates the 'nad_old_entry' field in the NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY notification with the overwritten entry's information. This is in accordance with RFC 8881bis Section 27.4.6. The test creates two files in a delegated directory, renames one file over the other, and then checks that the server sends a correct RENAME notifcation with the old entry's info.

series_items.subject: [PATCH pynfs v3 26/26] nfs4.1: move a lot of log/log_cb.info messages to log/log_cb.debug
series_items.patch_summary: This patch reduces the verbosity of a Linux NFS4 client by lowering the logging level of numerous informational messages from 'info' to 'debug'. This change is intended to minimize unnecessary log output in normal operation, while still allowing for detailed debugging when needed. The modifications affect several files within the nfs4.1 module, specifically altering the logging behavior in response to various events and operations. Overall, this patch aims to strike a balance between providing useful diagnostic information and avoiding excessive log noise.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds several test cases to the NFSv4.1 server testsuite, specifically focusing on directory delegation recall scenarios. The patches introduce new test functions for rename, link, and remove operations that trigger dir delegation recalls.

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton
review_comments.summary: The patches seem to be implementing the correct functionality for testing dir delegation recall scenarios. However, it's unclear if these tests are sufficient or if there are any edge cases that need to be considered.
review_comments.raw_body:
This flag has been proposed as part of RFC8881bis. This flag is used to
negotiate extensions to the original directory delegations originally
specified in RFC8881.

In practice, the Linux nfs server requires that the client support this
flag if it's requesting anything other than a recall-only delegation.

Signed-off-by: Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>
---
 nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x | 3 ++-
 1 file changed, 2 insertions(+), 1 deletion(-)

diff --git a/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x b/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x
index ee3da8aa7a34..f03eb538a298 100644
--- a/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x
+++ b/nfs4.1/xdrdef/nfs4.x
@@ -3611,7 +3611,8 @@ enum notify_type4 {
         NOTIFY4_REMOVE_ENTRY = 2,
         NOTIFY4_ADD_ENTRY = 3,
         NOTIFY4_RENAME_ENTRY = 4,
-        NOTIFY4_CHANGE_COOKIE_VERIFIER = 5
+        NOTIFY4_CHANGE_COOKIE_VERIFIER = 5,
+        NOTIFY4_GFLAG_EXTEND = 6 /* proposed in rfc8881bis */
 };
 
 /* Changed entry information.  */

-- 
2.54.0

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 0/6] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 0/6] nfsd/sunrpc: convert nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 1/6] sunrpc: add per-netns per-procedure call counts to svc_stat
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-network namespace (netns) and per-procedure call counts to the svc_stat structure in the sunrpc module. The existing global call count arrays are not network-namespace-aware, which is a problem for the upcoming netlink stats interface that requires namespace-scoped statistics. To solve this issue, the patch introduces per-netns equivalents of the global call count arrays and manages them using new alloc and free helpers. This change allows for more accurate and detailed statistics to be collected and reported.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 2/6] sunrpc: use per-net counts in svc_seq_show()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the svc_seq_show() function to read procedure call counts from per-network-namespace arrays instead of a global array. This change allows /proc/net/rpc/nfsd to report procedure call counts for each network namespace separately, which is useful for monitoring and debugging purposes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 3/6] nfsd: implement server-stats-get netlink handler
series_items.patch_summary: This patch implements a netlink handler to expose NFS server statistics, allowing them to be retrieved via the generic netlink family. The handler uses a dump operation to stream statistics across multiple messages, including scalar stats and per-version procedure counts. It also includes support for retrieving NFSv4 per-operation counts. This allows nfsstat to retrieve server statistics via netlink with a procfs fallback for older kernels.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Chuck Lever questioned the dump design, specifically asking about the handling of NFSv4 per-operation counts in subsequent messages, and requested clarification on how the current operation index is tracked across dump calls.

The reviewer pointed out that the patch does not implement a streaming design for netlink messages, as described in the commit message. Instead, it emits all proc4ops-ops nests in the first message, contradicting the code's own kerneldoc documentation.

The reviewer questioned whether the handler's strategy of assembling the entire server-stats object in one message under certain conditions would still work when the assembled message no longer fits the dump buffer, citing concerns about skb allocation and potential overflow.

Reviewer noted that the code for NFSD_A_SERVER_STATS_PROC4OPS_OPS in nfsd_nl_server_stats_get_dumpit() duplicates the same nest built by nfsd_nl_fill_proc_ops(), and suggested generalizing a helper to take the per-op counter source. Additionally, reviewer questioned whether it's intentional to emit entries for zero-count ops and suggested skipping them to trim message size. Lastly, reviewer pointed out that /proc/net/rpc/nfsd prints wdeleg_getattr statistics not exposed in the netlink dump.

The reviewer noted that when an nla_put operation fails, it leads to a terminal error being delivered to userspace instead of a 'buffer full' indication, causing the dump to fail due to oversized messages. The reviewer suggested building the dump in the same way as nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit(), with one entry per message and saving the cursor in cb->args.
review_comments.raw_body:
A couple of questions on the dump design and one on the commit message,
then some smaller items.

The commit message says:

---

The code does not stream across subsequent messages.  The proc4ops-ops
nests are emitted in the first message, inside the same "if (start == 0)"
block as the scalars, and cb->args[0] only ever holds 0 or -1, never an
operation index.  The function's own kerneldoc agrees: "The entire
server-stats object is emitted in a single netlink message on the first
invocation."

Should the commit message be reworded to match the code, or should the code
move to the streaming design the message describes?  That second option ties
into the next question.

---

This handler assembles the entire server-stats object -- all scalars, the
proc2/3/4-ops nests, and one proc4ops-ops nest per NFSv4 operation -- in one
message under "if (start == 0)", then sets cb->args[0] = -1 so the next call
ends the dump.

Does that hold up once the assembled message no longer fits the dump buffer?
A dump skb is allocated at max(cb->min_dump_alloc, NLMSG_GOODSIZE), and this
handler sets neither cb->min_dump_alloc nor a .start callback, so the floor
is NLMSG_GOODSIZE, which is SKB_WITH_OVERHEAD(PAGE_SIZE), roughly 3.7k on a
4k-page host.  A larger skb is allocated only when userspace has previously
grown its receive buffer.

With LAST_NFS4_OP at 75, the proc4ops-ops loop alone emits 76 nests of about
28 bytes each (~2.1k); the proc2 and proc3 nests plus the scalar block bring
the total to roughly 3.5k -- already within a few hundred bytes of the floor,
and growing by one nest for every NFSv4 operation added later.

[ ... scalar puts for reply cache, fh, io, net, rpc snipped ... ]

---

This loop open-codes the same nest that nfsd_nl_fill_proc_ops() builds just
above -- nla_nest_start(), nla_put_u32(PROC_ENTRY_OP),
nla_put_u64_64bit(PROC_ENTRY_COUNT), nla_nest_end() -- into the same
NFSD_A_SERVER_STATS_PROC4OPS_OPS attribute.  Could the helper be generalized
to take the per-op counter source so this is not a second copy of the same
code?

The per-version block above skips empty versions:

	if (!prog->pg_vers[i] || !statp->vs_count[i])
		continue;

but this loop emits an entry for every op 0..LAST_NFS4_OP, zero-count ops
included.  Is that difference intentional?  Skipping zero counts here would
also trim the worst-case message size above.

There is also a counter that this dump does not emit.  /proc/net/rpc/nfsd
prints a wdeleg_getattr line after proc4ops:

	seq_printf(seq, "\nwdeleg_getattr %lld",
		percpu_counter_sum_positive(&nn->counter[NFSD_STATS_WDELEG_GETATTR]));

incremented by nfsd_stats_wdeleg_getattr_inc().  Since the goal is to expose
the statistics currently available via /proc/net/rpc/nfsd, should
wdeleg_getattr get an attribute here too, so nfsstat over netlink does not
drop it relative to the procfs path?

---

Back to the buffer question: when an nla_put eventually fails, control
reaches err_cancel, and genlmsg_cancel() trims the partial message back out,
so skb->len is 0 on return.  In netlink_dump():

	if (nlk->dump_done_errno == -EMSGSIZE && skb->len)
		nlk->dump_done_errno = skb->len;

the skb->len test fails, so -EMSGSIZE is delivered to userspace as a
terminal error rather than being taken as "buffer full, call again".  With
everything emitted under "if (start == 0)" and no per-attribute cursor in
cb->args, a retry rebuilds the same oversized message, so the dump cannot
ever complete.

nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit() already follows the streaming pattern the
commit message describes: one entry per message, saving the cursor in
cb->args and returning skb->len on EMSGSIZE.  Would building this dump the
same way be more robust than the single-message form?

-- 
Chuck Lever

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 4/6] sunrpc: remove unused svc_version vs_count field
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the unused vs_count field from struct svc_version and its associated percpu counters across various NFS-related modules, as it is no longer accessed by any code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 5/6] nfsd: count NFSv4 callback operations per netns
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-netns counters for NFSv4 callback operations in the Linux kernel's nfsd module. It introduces a new array of percpu counters, indexed by RFC 8881 callback opcode, to track the number of callbacks sent to clients. The relevant counter is bumped in the nfsd4_run_cb() function, which is called once per queued callback. This change also updates the procfs statistics and removes redundant code related to write delegation conflicts.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the code captures the cb_ops pointer and reads ops->opcode after nfsd4_queue_cb() returns, which is safe because every cb_ops points to a file-scope static const nfsd4_callback_ops structure that outlives cb. The reviewer suggested rephrasing the comment to reflect that it's snapshotting the cb_ops pointer rather than the opcode.

Reviewer noted that the increment of OP_CB_SEQUENCE counter fires for callbacks that never send a CB_SEQUENCE, specifically null probes and client teardowns on minorversion > 0 clients, and suggested moving the increment inside the if (ops) block to avoid this issue.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 6/6] nfsd: export NFSv4 callback op stats via netlink
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds the ability to export NFSv4 callback operation statistics via netlink, allowing nfsstat to report counts of backchannel operations over netlink. The stats are added as a nested attribute in the server-stats netlink dump, reusing the existing layout for scalar stats. This includes counting CB_GETATTR operations, which corresponds to the procfs wdeleg_getattr line.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series converts the nfsstat server-side interfaces to use netlink instead of /proc/net/rpc/nfsd, which has several limitations. The new implementation adds per-network-namespace procedure call counts and exposes them through a new netlink handler in the nfsd generic netlink family. It also tracks callback operation counts and provides a procfs fallback for older kernels. The patch series includes six patches that make changes to various files in the kernel, including sunrpc, nfsd, and lockd.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the netlink handler in nfsd_netlink.c does not properly handle errors when sending a server-stats object, and requested that error handling be added to ensure that an error is returned when nla_put_*() calls fail.

Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the netlink handler does not stream across subsequent messages, instead emitting all server-stats objects in a single message on the first invocation, which contradicts the commit message's description of streaming design.

The reviewer, Chuck Lever, pointed out that the server-stats object assembly in one message could lead to a buffer overflow issue when the assembled message exceeds the dump buffer size of NLMSG_GOODSIZE (approximately 3.7k on a 4k-page host). He noted that with LAST_NFS4_OP at 75, the proc4ops-ops loop alone emits around 2.1k bytes, and adding more NFSv4 operations would further increase the total size, potentially exceeding the buffer limit.

The reviewer pointed out that the patch duplicates code from nfsd_nl_fill_proc_ops() and suggested generalizing a helper function to take the per-op counter source, reducing code duplication. Additionally, they noted that the loop emits entries for zero-count ops, which may not be intentional, and requested consideration of including the wdeleg_getattr counter in the netlink dump.

Reviewer noted that when an nla_put fails, genlmsg_cancel() trims the partial message and skb->len becomes 0, causing -EMSGSIZE to be delivered as a terminal error instead of indicating a buffer full condition. He suggested building the dump in the same streaming pattern used by nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit(), where one entry is emitted per message with the cursor saved in cb->args.
sentiment_signals: contradiction between code and commit message
review_comments.raw_body:
A couple of questions on the dump design and one on the commit message,
then some smaller items.

The commit message says:

---

The code does not stream across subsequent messages.  The proc4ops-ops
nests are emitted in the first message, inside the same "if (start == 0)"
block as the scalars, and cb->args[0] only ever holds 0 or -1, never an
operation index.  The function's own kerneldoc agrees: "The entire
server-stats object is emitted in a single netlink message on the first
invocation."

Should the commit message be reworded to match the code, or should the code
move to the streaming design the message describes?  That second option ties
into the next question.

---

This handler assembles the entire server-stats object -- all scalars, the
proc2/3/4-ops nests, and one proc4ops-ops nest per NFSv4 operation -- in one
message under "if (start == 0)", then sets cb->args[0] = -1 so the next call
ends the dump.

Does that hold up once the assembled message no longer fits the dump buffer?
A dump skb is allocated at max(cb->min_dump_alloc, NLMSG_GOODSIZE), and this
handler sets neither cb->min_dump_alloc nor a .start callback, so the floor
is NLMSG_GOODSIZE, which is SKB_WITH_OVERHEAD(PAGE_SIZE), roughly 3.7k on a
4k-page host.  A larger skb is allocated only when userspace has previously
grown its receive buffer.

With LAST_NFS4_OP at 75, the proc4ops-ops loop alone emits 76 nests of about
28 bytes each (~2.1k); the proc2 and proc3 nests plus the scalar block bring
the total to roughly 3.5k -- already within a few hundred bytes of the floor,
and growing by one nest for every NFSv4 operation added later.

[ ... scalar puts for reply cache, fh, io, net, rpc snipped ... ]

---

This loop open-codes the same nest that nfsd_nl_fill_proc_ops() builds just
above -- nla_nest_start(), nla_put_u32(PROC_ENTRY_OP),
nla_put_u64_64bit(PROC_ENTRY_COUNT), nla_nest_end() -- into the same
NFSD_A_SERVER_STATS_PROC4OPS_OPS attribute.  Could the helper be generalized
to take the per-op counter source so this is not a second copy of the same
code?

The per-version block above skips empty versions:

	if (!prog->pg_vers[i] || !statp->vs_count[i])
		continue;

but this loop emits an entry for every op 0..LAST_NFS4_OP, zero-count ops
included.  Is that difference intentional?  Skipping zero counts here would
also trim the worst-case message size above.

There is also a counter that this dump does not emit.  /proc/net/rpc/nfsd
prints a wdeleg_getattr line after proc4ops:

	seq_printf(seq, "\nwdeleg_getattr %lld",
		percpu_counter_sum_positive(&nn->counter[NFSD_STATS_WDELEG_GETATTR]));

incremented by nfsd_stats_wdeleg_getattr_inc().  Since the goal is to expose
the statistics currently available via /proc/net/rpc/nfsd, should
wdeleg_getattr get an attribute here too, so nfsstat over netlink does not
drop it relative to the procfs path?

---

Back to the buffer question: when an nla_put eventually fails, control
reaches err_cancel, and genlmsg_cancel() trims the partial message back out,
so skb->len is 0 on return.  In netlink_dump():

	if (nlk->dump_done_errno == -EMSGSIZE && skb->len)
		nlk->dump_done_errno = skb->len;

the skb->len test fails, so -EMSGSIZE is delivered to userspace as a
terminal error rather than being taken as "buffer full, call again".  With
everything emitted under "if (start == 0)" and no per-attribute cursor in
cb->args, a retry rebuilds the same oversized message, so the dump cannot
ever complete.

nfsd_nl_rpc_status_get_dumpit() already follows the streaming pattern the
commit message describes: one entry per message, saving the cursor in
cb->args and returning skb->len on EMSGSIZE.  Would building this dump the
same way be more robust than the single-message form?

-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer noted that the code captures the cb_ops pointer and reads ops->opcode after nfsd4_queue_cb() returns, which is safe because every cb_ops points at a file-scope static const nfsd4_callback_ops structure that outlives cb. The reviewer suggested that the comment would read more accurately as snapshotting the cb_ops pointer rather than the opcode.

Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the increment of OP_CB_SEQUENCE count is triggered by minorversion > 0, including cases where no CB_SEQUENCE is sent on the wire, such as callback-channel probes and client teardown. He suggested moving the increment inside the if (ops) block to avoid this issue.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 01/10] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching and sending the next request in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver from io_uring, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. The duplicated logic in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() is removed, and a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() is introduced to fetch the next request without sending it immediately. This change simplifies the code and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 02/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which simplifies the code and centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging. The changes affect several functions in fs/fuse/dev_uring.c, making it easier to maintain and modify the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 03/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts this logic into a new function called copy_header_from_ring(), making it clearer and more centralized, and also improves error handling and logging.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 04/10] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE file system by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This simplifies both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying, particularly when buffer rings are added. The patch introduces an enum type `fuse_uring_header_type` with values for different header types and modifies existing functions to use this new type. The changes improve code readability and maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 05/10] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) driver, specifically for payload copying. A new helper function called `setup_fuse_copy_state` is introduced to encapsulate this logic, making the code more modular and easier to maintain. The function takes several parameters, including a pointer to the copy state structure, a fuse ring, a request, an entry in the ring, and an iterator. It initializes the copy state and imports user buffers as needed. This patch is part of a larger series of changes to improve the FUSE driver's performance and reliability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 06/10] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices 0 and 1 in the iovec array with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support. The changes are made in the fuse:dev_uring.c file, specifically in the functions fuse_uring_get_iovec_from_sqe and fuse_uring_create_ring_ent. This patch is part of a larger series of 10 patches and has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass in a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used as needed by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. A buffer ring data structure is used to manage the buffer pool, selecting and recycling buffers on demand.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi noted that the registration interface for FUSE io-uring buffer pools is inconsistent, as it decouples buffers from sqe's but not the registration process itself. He suggested adding a new cmd op (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_BUFPOOL_SETUP) to register the buffer pool independently of sqe setup.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> payload in the first sqe.

I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?

Thanks,
Miklos

---

On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> Buffer ring request flow
> ------------------------
> |  Kernel                                  |  FUSE daemon
> |                                          |
> |  [client request arrives]                |
> |  >fuse_uring_send()                      |
> |    [select payload buf from pool]        |
> |    >fuse_uring_select_buffer()           |
> |    [copy headers to ent]                 |
> |    >copy_header_to_ring()                |
> |    [copy payload to selected buf]        |
> |    >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()            |
> |    [set buf_id in ent_in_out header]     |
> |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                  |
> |                                          |  [CQE received]
> |                                          |  [read headers]
> |                                          |  [read payload from buf_id]

How does buf_id map to a buffer address?

Wouldn't it be simpler to provide the buffer address directly?

We also need some uAPI documentation (generally not just for this
patchset).  What about a new directory:
Documentation/filesystems/fuse/uapi?

Thanks,
Milklos

---

On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 at 07:38, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
> allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
> compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.

But which buffer?   The interface is supposed to handle multiple
pools, in which case the server needs to find the pool and the buffer
within the pool.

FIXED ops also require buf_index.  Things are getting complicated...

> This would have to be returned as part of the struct
> fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
> use.

Does the 12 bytes limit come from the cqe?

> Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
> using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
> buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
> trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
> the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
> good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
> offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
> would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.

Yeah, offset sounds good, but still need to select between the pools.
So should that be the "pool index" that would be the same as
"buf_index" for registered buffers?

Thanks,
Miklos


Thanks,
Miklos

On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 at 07:28, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
> >
> > On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > > To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> > > registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> > > payload in the first sqe.
> >
> > I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
> > the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?
>
> Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
> want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
> registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
> up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
> (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
> number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in.

Why not a separate op for bufpool registration, so it can be done
independently of the setup, possibly at some later time?

Thanks,
Miklos

---

On Wed, 17 Jun 2026 at 14:50, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> I'm not really convinced there's a compelling use case for needing
> multiple bufpools in one queue. This was the discussion with Bernd in
> [1] about this. In my opinion, incremental buffer consumption and
> allowing different queues to support different payload sizes would
> address the use cases of multiple bufpools within one queue, but would
> be simpler.

Confused.  I thought this was exactly about multiple bufpools
(containing different size bufs) per ring.

If not, then what's the point of dynamically assigning bufs to sqe's?

Thanks,
Miklos

---

On Thu, 18 Jun 2026 at 09:03, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> imo the main benefit is for incremental buffer consumption (allowing
> non-overlapping parts of a buffer to be used by multiple entries
> concurrently). To recycle back a buffer that is used by multiple
> entries, it would require all requests to have been completed but I
> don't think in practice this is a bottleneck with reasonably sized
> buffer pools across queues. imo incremental buffer consumption scales
> more dynamically with runtime workloads than preallocating fixed
> buffers of different sizes across different queues trying to
> anticipate / guess workload needs. Overprovisioning small buffers
> wastes memory if those requests never appear, while splitting the
> memory budget towards smaller pools reduces capacity for concurrent
> large requests, and workload patterns might need either at different
> times.

Hmm, so why partition this memory into buffers up front?

We could just as well say that managing that chunk of  memory is up to
the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?

> I think we should still make the design for bufpools
> forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
> comes up in the future,

Yeah, and separating buffer registration into a separate op would be
one step in that direction.   It doesn't have to support multiple
bufpools from the start, but splitting out separate functions during
initialization is a good idea regardless.

> From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
> different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
> requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
> requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests.

Note, "queue" could mean two things here:

 - fuse's waiting list
 - cqe's on the ring

Currently there's 1:1 relation between those, but that's not mandatory
going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing Miklos' concerns about the interface for handling multiple buffer pools, specifically how the server can compute the address of a buffer given its ID and pool information. The author proposes adding a 'uint16_t pool_idx' to struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out to indicate which pool a buffer belongs to, but notes that this would require userspace to keep track of the starting address of each pool.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> > registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> > payload in the first sqe.
>
> I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
> the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?

Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
(FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of
right now creating the queue lazily when the first sqe gets
registered. On the userspace side, this adds a bit more friction (eg a
two-phase setup where userspace submits the queue setup cmd, reaps its
completion, and reuses that sqe to register an entry), but that seems
like a reasonable tradeoff.

Does this sound ok to you and Bernd?

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260529-reduced-nr-ring-queues_3-v5-0-1dc08c2fccf6@bsbernd.com/T/#u

>
> Thanks,
> Miklos

---

On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 5:33 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > Buffer ring request flow
> > ------------------------
> > |  Kernel                                  |  FUSE daemon
> > |                                          |
> > |  [client request arrives]                |
> > |  >fuse_uring_send()                      |
> > |    [select payload buf from pool]        |
> > |    >fuse_uring_select_buffer()           |
> > |    [copy headers to ent]                 |
> > |    >copy_header_to_ring()                |
> > |    [copy payload to selected buf]        |
> > |    >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()            |
> > |    [set buf_id in ent_in_out header]     |
> > |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                  |
> > |                                          |  [CQE received]
> > |                                          |  [read headers]
> > |                                          |  [read payload from buf_id]
>
> How does buf_id map to a buffer address?

The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.

>
> Wouldn't it be simpler to provide the buffer address directly?

This would have to be returned as part of the struct
fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
use. Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.

>
> We also need some uAPI documentation (generally not just for this
> patchset).  What about a new directory:
> Documentation/filesystems/fuse/uapi?

That sounds good. I'll write some uAPI documentation for this and add
it to that new directory.

Thanks,
Joanne

---

On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 6:22 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 at 07:38, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
> > allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
> > compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.
>
> But which buffer?   The interface is supposed to handle multiple
> pools, in which case the server needs to find the pool and the buffer
> within the pool.

If we later wanted to add support for multiple buffer pools, we would
need to extend the struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out to include a "uint16_t
pool_idx" to indicate which pool. Userspace would be responsible for
keeping track of the starting address of each pool.

>
> FIXED ops also require buf_index.  Things are getting complicated...

For FIXED ops not through zero-copy, there's one index for the entire
pool (eg this is the index where the pool resides). It passes this by
setting the sqe->buf_index.
For FIXED ops through zero-copy, userspace is required to register a
sparse buffer where the kernel should place the application's/page
cache's pages. Userspace determines the index into this sparse buffer
for the sqe and it passes this index to the kernel in the init setup
in the "uint16_t zero_copy_index;" field when it registers the sqe.

Neither require the kernel to pass back a buf index corresponding to
the fixed buffer index. For both cases, userspace knows the index for
the fixed buffer (it is the one who determined it) and it is the one
who passes this info to the kernel at setup time.

>
> > This would have to be returned as part of the struct
> > fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
> > use.
>
> Does the 12 bytes limit come from the cqe?

The 12 byte limit comes from the "struct fuse_uring_ent_in_out"
definition in the uapi [1].

>
> > Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
> > using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
> > buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
> > trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
> > the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
> > good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
> > offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
> > would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.
>
> Yeah, offset sounds good, but still need to select between the pools.

For multiple buffer pools, I was thinking that would be through a
separate "uint16_t pool_idx" field to indicate which pool.

> So should that be the "pool index" that would be the same as
> "buf_index" for registered buffers?

I think we should just pass back "pool index" and have userspace keep
track of the registered buffer id that corresponds to that pool index.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.1/source/include/uapi/linux/fuse.h#L1255

---

On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 6:26 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 at 07:28, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
> > >
> > > On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> > >
> > > > To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> > > > registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> > > > payload in the first sqe.
> > >
> > > I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
> > > the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?
> >
> > Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
> > want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
> > registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
> > up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
> > (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
> > number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in.
>
> Why not a separate op for bufpool registration, so it can be done
> independently of the setup, possibly at some later time?

I'm not really convinced there's a compelling use case for needing
multiple bufpools in one queue. This was the discussion with Bernd in
[1] about this. In my opinion, incremental buffer consumption and
allowing different queues to support different payload sizes would
address the use cases of multiple bufpools within one queue, but would
be simpler.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/CAJnrk1Yw3=z7W_my4pLG6avBeDZkUp3j1LZk-RVpGv2vAsw-ZA@mail.gmail.com/

> >>> registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> >>> payload in the first sqe.
> >>
> >> I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
> >> the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?
> >
> > Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
> > want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
> > registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
> > up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
> > (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
> > number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
> > nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of
> > right now creating the queue lazily when the first sqe gets
> > registered. On the userspace side, this adds a bit more friction (eg a
> > two-phase setup where userspace submits the queue setup cmd, reaps its
> > completion, and reuses that sqe to register an entry), but that seems
> > like a reasonable tradeoff.
> >
> > Does this sound ok to you and Bernd?
>
> Can we please discuss this on Thursday? I'm by no means opposed to a

Definitely. Discussing this on Thursday sounds like a great idea

> config SQE, but I don't see how it will really help. My issue is that
> any fixed number of queues and queue entries is almost never the right one.

I agree. I definitely think it would be useful to add more queue/ent
capacity dynamically, but imo I think that should be done separately
through a new cmd op after uring setup. I think it'd save some
headache to keep setup and runtime resizing as separate concerns /
paths and would be easier to reason about different races that could
happen and would keep the setup logic simpler.

> The config setting (via SQE, ioctl or FUSE_INIT) could be used to
> prepare the kernel, but final routing update needs to be dynamic.

Agreed that final routing update would be dynamic.

Thanks,
Joanne

On Wed, Jun 17, 2026 at 10:07 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Wed, 17 Jun 2026 at 14:50, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > I'm not really convinced there's a compelling use case for needing
> > multiple bufpools in one queue. This was the discussion with Bernd in
> > [1] about this. In my opinion, incremental buffer consumption and
> > allowing different queues to support different payload sizes would
> > address the use cases of multiple bufpools within one queue, but would
> > be simpler.
>
> Confused.  I thought this was exactly about multiple bufpools
> (containing different size bufs) per ring.
>
> If not, then what's the point of dynamically assigning bufs to sqe's?

imo the main benefit is for incremental buffer consumption (allowing
non-overlapping parts of a buffer to be used by multiple entries
concurrently). To recycle back a buffer that is used by multiple
entries, it would require all requests to have been completed but I
don't think in practice this is a bottleneck with reasonably sized
buffer pools across queues. imo incremental buffer consumption scales
more dynamically with runtime workloads than preallocating fixed
buffers of different sizes across different queues trying to
anticipate / guess workload needs. Overprovisioning small buffers
wastes memory if those requests never appear, while splitting the
memory budget towards smaller pools reduces capacity for concurrent
large requests, and workload patterns might need either at different
times.

I think we should still make the design for bufpools
forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
comes up in the future, but I think for the majority of
servers/workloads, incremental buffer consumption would meet their
needs and be simpler logic wise and avoids the server having to try to
figure out how to optimally distribute different size bufpools across
differnet queues.

>From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests. The non-IO
queues would only need small buffers (eg <= 4k). Userspace could try
implementing separation with threadpools instead of fuse uring doing
queue-level separation, but I think this adds extra context-switching
overhead and is a lot more complex to get performant. If a server
truly needs multiple buffer sizes beyond just non-IO vs IO separation,
I think having multiple bufpools per queue would be a better choice
than dedicating queues to specific categories of buffer sizes; in the
initial discussion, I had assumed the buffer search overhead /
complexity would be worse with per-queue multiple bufpools, but with
the free lists you mentioned, I think you could do the same thing here
with a free list at the fc level for bufpools across all queues, and
that would address the search concern.

Thanks,
Joanne

---

> >>> registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
> >>> up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
> >>> (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
> >>> number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in.
> >>
> >> Why not a separate op for bufpool registration, so it can be done
> >> independently of the setup, possibly at some later time?

> > I'm not really convinced there's a compelling use case for needing
> > multiple bufpools in one queue. This was the discussion with Bernd in
> > [1] about this. In my opinion, incremental buffer consumption and
> > allowing different queues to support different payload sizes would
> > address the use cases of multiple bufpools within one queue, but would
> > be simpler.
>
> To be honest, I have no idea how to implement queues with different IO
> sizes. What would be the logic to select a queue? And what is if we

With the separation between non-IO queues and IO-queues, I think we
could just look at the request type, and dispatch accordingly so that
the metadata ops go to the non-io queues and the read/write ops go to
the io queues. I think the dispatch logic would be otherwise the same
for choosing which particular queue within the non-io or io queues.

> start with an single max-IO buf and a single queue (per numa) and then
> scale up based on need? Let's say we have:
>
> Start 1 queue (per numa maybe), one max payload buf in that queue and
> allow to scale up to max nr CPUs for the cpu to queue-id mapping. But
> now additionally we get for the same CPU
>
> - multiple queues of the same buf size
> - multiple queues of different buf sizes
>

I'm not seeing why this would be an issue. For multiple queues of the
same buffer size, it would just be normal queue selection and for
different buffer sizes, we'd jjust have two pools of queues (non-io
and io) and select based on request type first which pool then apply
normal queue selection within that pool. I think if a server has needs
for multiple categories of buffer sizes beyond just non-io vs io,
having multiple bufpools per queue makes more sense than dedicated
queues to the specific categories of the different buffer sizes, but I
doubt this level of granularity would be needed.

>
> Also, every queue has its own ring, syscall aggregation is not given
> anymore.

Syscall aggregation still applies within each ring, but I don't think
sysclal aggregation is really a big factor given that libfuse and the
server handles requests as they arrive and respond as soon as the
request is handled, instead of batching multiple responses before
submitting; on the dispatch side, requests are sent one by one as they
come in rather than batched, so there's no aggregation to lose.

Thanks,
Joanne

We already have logic for this (done in your reduced queue series). I
think we could just reuse that logic. A basic implementation would be
having a separate bitmap for non-IO queues vs IO queues, and then once
you figure out if the request is an IO or non-IO request, the rest of

the non-IO queue pool or the IO queue pool. If you're concerned about
dispatch complexity, I think separate bufpools per queue ends up being
more complicated.

I really don't think servers will start with 1 buffer per queue.

Happy to discuss this together in the call later today.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addresses Miklos Szeredi's concern about partitioning memory into buffers up front and simplifying the interface by having the kernel manage the buffer pool. The author agrees that the buffer size is redundant and could be removed, but notes that adding bufsize to the uapi would make it forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools per queue in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jun 18, 2026 at 1:37 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Thu, 18 Jun 2026 at 09:03, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > imo the main benefit is for incremental buffer consumption (allowing
> > non-overlapping parts of a buffer to be used by multiple entries
> > concurrently). To recycle back a buffer that is used by multiple
> > entries, it would require all requests to have been completed but I
> > don't think in practice this is a bottleneck with reasonably sized
> > buffer pools across queues. imo incremental buffer consumption scales
> > more dynamically with runtime workloads than preallocating fixed
> > buffers of different sizes across different queues trying to
> > anticipate / guess workload needs. Overprovisioning small buffers
> > wastes memory if those requests never appear, while splitting the
> > memory budget towards smaller pools reduces capacity for concurrent
> > large requests, and workload patterns might need either at different
> > times.
>
> Hmm, so why partition this memory into buffers up front?
>
> We could just as well say that managing that chunk of  memory is up to
> the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
> redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?

That's a good point, especially since we can just return back offset
into the bufpools instead of buffer id. That's a much cleaner
interface. Though I guess if we want to be forwards-compatible with
having the option of multiple bufpools per queue in the future, we'd
have to add bufsize to the uapi here now. Do you think we should just
keep it as is then?

>
> > I think we should still make the design for bufpools
> > forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
> > comes up in the future,
>
> Yeah, and separating buffer registration into a separate op would be
> one step in that direction.   It doesn't have to support multiple
> bufpools from the start, but splitting out separate functions during
> initialization is a good idea regardless.

I'm not sure I agree that splitting it out would be a good idea if we
don't ever end up supporting multiple bufpools (imo, having this in
QUEUE_SETUP would logically make sense and be more ergonomic) but I'm
happy to make this change.

>
> > From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
> > different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
> > requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
> > requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests.
>
> Note, "queue" could mean two things here:
>
>  - fuse's waiting list
>  - cqe's on the ring
>
> Currently there's 1:1 relation between those, but that's not mandatory
> going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
> types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.

Even with separate waiting queues and the kernel prioritizing sending
metadata requests before io, isn't there the issue that if the
requests/entries on the ring are all IO requests and then a storm of
metadata ops come in, they're still backed up waiting for the IO
requests to be finished? Or am I missing something?

On the userspace side, I think they'd need a threadpool which would
add extra context-switching overhead and would have to reserve a few
threads in that pool just for non-IO requests, and then have a way to
inspect requests in the ring to prioritize the non-IO ones, which
seems nontrivial. But maybe there's some simpler way to do this that
I'm not seeing.

Thanks,
Joanne

---

On Thu, Jun 18, 2026 at 10:20 AM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Thu, Jun 18, 2026 at 1:37 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
> >
> > On Thu, 18 Jun 2026 at 09:03, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > > imo the main benefit is for incremental buffer consumption (allowing
> > > non-overlapping parts of a buffer to be used by multiple entries
> > > concurrently). To recycle back a buffer that is used by multiple
> > > entries, it would require all requests to have been completed but I
> > > don't think in practice this is a bottleneck with reasonably sized
> > > buffer pools across queues. imo incremental buffer consumption scales
> > > more dynamically with runtime workloads than preallocating fixed
> > > buffers of different sizes across different queues trying to
> > > anticipate / guess workload needs. Overprovisioning small buffers
> > > wastes memory if those requests never appear, while splitting the
> > > memory budget towards smaller pools reduces capacity for concurrent
> > > large requests, and workload patterns might need either at different
> > > times.
> >
> > Hmm, so why partition this memory into buffers up front?
> >
> > We could just as well say that managing that chunk of  memory is up to
> > the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
> > redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?
>
> That's a good point, especially since we can just return back offset
> into the bufpools instead of buffer id. That's a much cleaner
> interface. Though I guess if we want to be forwards-compatible with
> having the option of multiple bufpools per queue in the future, we'd
> have to add bufsize to the uapi here now. Do you think we should just
> keep it as is then?

Ignore the last two sentences please, I didn't think through your
original message.

I like your idea a lot about just passing a chunk of memory to the
kernel and offloading the management logic entirely to the kernel.
Maybe I'm missing something obvious, but why would multiple pools per
queue still be needed? If the server needs more capacity, why not just
have the server spin up a new queue? That would have the included
benefit of adding more concurrency, and dynamically adding more queues
is a feature that would be added to fuse uring regardless. For
multipools in one queue, the pools would be discontiguous anyways,
which wouldn't help with fragmentation. I don't think adding a queue
incurs that much memory or cpu overhead, and imo the scenarios where a
server would want to just add more capacity but not parallelism are
uncommon.

>
> >
> > > I think we should still make the design for bufpools
> > > forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
> > > comes up in the future,
> >
> > Yeah, and separating buffer registration into a separate op would be
> > one step in that direction.   It doesn't have to support multiple
> > bufpools from the start, but splitting out separate functions during
> > initialization is a good idea regardless.
>
> I'm not sure I agree that splitting it out would be a good idea if we
> don't ever end up supporting multiple bufpools (imo, having this in
> QUEUE_SETUP would logically make sense and be more ergonomic) but I'm
> happy to make this change.
>
> >
> > > From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
> > > different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
> > > requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
> > > requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests.
> >
> > Note, "queue" could mean two things here:
> >
> >  - fuse's waiting list
> >  - cqe's on the ring
> >
> > Currently there's 1:1 relation between those, but that's not mandatory
> > going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
> > types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.
>
> Even with separate waiting queues and the kernel prioritizing sending
> metadata requests before io, isn't there the issue that if the
> requests/entries on the ring are all IO requests and then a storm of
> metadata ops come in, they're still backed up waiting for the IO
> requests to be finished? Or am I missing something?

Thinking about this some more, I think the kernel could just
pre-reserve some ring entries specifically for metadata ops / non-IO
requests. That solves it on the kernel side but the userspace side
would still need a threadpool of some sort if its backend IO blocks
its queue thread.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Bernd Schubert expressed concerns about the buffer pool interface, specifically that it is inconsistent to decouple buffers from sqe registration but not the registration itself. He suggested adding a new cmd op (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) for queue-level settings, including flags and number of queues, which would also allow for dynamic routing updates.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 6/16/26 07:28, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41\u202fAM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>>
>> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>>> To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
>>> registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
>>> payload in the first sqe.
>>
>> I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
>> the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?
> 
> Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
> want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
> registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
> up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
> (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
> number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
> nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of
> right now creating the queue lazily when the first sqe gets
> registered. On the userspace side, this adds a bit more friction (eg a
> two-phase setup where userspace submits the queue setup cmd, reaps its
> completion, and reuses that sqe to register an entry), but that seems
> like a reasonable tradeoff.
> 
> Does this sound ok to you and Bernd?

Can we please discuss this on Thursday? I'm by no means opposed to a
config SQE, but I don't see how it will really help. My issue is that
any fixed number of queues and queue entries is almost never the right one.
The config setting (via SQE, ioctl or FUSE_INIT) could be used to
prepare the kernel, but final routing update needs to be dynamic.


Thanks,
Bernd

---

>> Hmm, so why partition this memory into buffers up front?
>>
>> We could just as well say that managing that chunk of  memory is up to
>> the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
>> redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?
> 
> That's a good point, especially since we can just return back offset
> into the bufpools instead of buffer id. That's a much cleaner
> interface. Though I guess if we want to be forwards-compatible with
> having the option of multiple bufpools per queue in the future, we'd
> have to add bufsize to the uapi here now. Do you think we should just
> keep it as is then?
> 
>>
>>> I think we should still make the design for bufpools
>>> forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
>>> comes up in the future,
>>
>> Yeah, and separating buffer registration into a separate op would be
>> one step in that direction.   It doesn't have to support multiple
>> bufpools from the start, but splitting out separate functions during
>> initialization is a good idea regardless.
> 
> I'm not sure I agree that splitting it out would be a good idea if we
> don't ever end up supporting multiple bufpools (imo, having this in
> QUEUE_SETUP would logically make sense and be more ergonomic) but I'm
> happy to make this change.
> 
>>
>>> From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
>>> different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
>>> requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
>>> requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests.
>>
>> Note, "queue" could mean two things here:
>>
>>  - fuse's waiting list
>>  - cqe's on the ring
>>
>> Currently there's 1:1 relation between those, but that's not mandatory
>> going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
>> types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.
> 
> Even with separate waiting queues and the kernel prioritizing sending
> metadata requests before io, isn't there the issue that if the
> requests/entries on the ring are all IO requests and then a storm of
> metadata ops come in, they're still backed up waiting for the IO
> requests to be finished? Or am I missing something?
> 
> On the userspace side, I think they'd need a threadpool which would
> add extra context-switching overhead and would have to reserve a few
> threads in that pool just for non-IO requests, and then have a way to
> inspect requests in the ring to prioritize the non-IO ones, which
> seems nontrivial. But maybe there's some simpler way to do this that
> I'm not seeing.

I personally do not see the big issue to resort CQEs on the server side
for priority. This is a just a list of CQEs and that can be scanned
which op codes are in them and if there is an optional priority flag.
For example to aggregate to larger buffers creating IOVs or to get
better alignment for EC this might be very useful even without prio (and
we have a modified patch from Jingbo applied, but there are still issues).

Thanks,
Bernd


Thanks,
Bernd

>>>> don't think in practice this is a bottleneck with reasonably sized
>>>> buffer pools across queues. imo incremental buffer consumption scales
>>>> more dynamically with runtime workloads than preallocating fixed
>>>> buffers of different sizes across different queues trying to
>>>> anticipate / guess workload needs. Overprovisioning small buffers
>>>> wastes memory if those requests never appear, while splitting the
>>>> memory budget towards smaller pools reduces capacity for concurrent
>>>> large requests, and workload patterns might need either at different
>>>> times.
>>>
>>> Hmm, so why partition this memory into buffers up front?

>>> We could just as well say that managing that chunk of  memory is up to
>>> the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
>>> redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?
>>

>> into the bufpools instead of buffer id. That's a much cleaner

> 
> Ignore the last two sentences please, I didn't think through your

> I like your idea a lot about just passing a chunk of memory to the

> Maybe I'm missing something obvious, but why would multiple pools per
> queue still be needed? If the server needs more capacity, why not just
> have the server spin up a new queue? That would have the included
> benefit of adding more concurrency, and dynamically adding more queues
> is a feature that would be added to fuse uring regardless. For
