> which wouldn't help with fragmentation. I don't think adding a queue
> incurs that much memory or cpu overhead, and imo the scenarios where a
> server would want to just add more capacity but not parallelism are
> uncommon.

How do you select the queue - is the queue now per CPU or is the queue
2D per CPU and per buffers? If per CPU, what is if you already have
queues on all CPUs and still want to add buffers?
If per CPU and buf, gets a 2D matrix - gets difficult to find a suitable
queue.
And even if queues are per CPU and enough free CPUs available - might
cause some non-optimal routing.

Maybe I miss some simple logic.

>>>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 08/10] fuse: support registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring for the fuse file system. It allows servers to use an io-uring registered buffer as the buffer pool, eliminating the overhead of pinning and unpinning user pages per I/O request. The server must register the payload buffers through io-uring and set specific flags during registration. This patch also includes kernel support for registered buffers advertised via a single umbrella flag. Benchmarks show a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads, 4% for direct sequential reads, 8% for buffered randreads, and 6% for buffered sequential reads.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi questioned the addition of a new parameter init_flags to the fuse_uring_bufpool_setup function without providing a specific reason or context.
review_comments.raw_body:
Is there any particular reason for this?  Not saying this is bad, just
wondering...

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern about pinned pages being accounted for and limited by the kernel io-uring infrastructure, confirming that this is indeed handled.

Author addressed Miklos Szeredi's concern that registered buffers should be supported without the buffer pool feature, explaining that it would add complexity and reduce test surface area. Author believes servers wanting registered buffers also want buffer pools, so no wanted use case is being left out.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, pinned pages are accounted and limited by the kernel io-uring
infrastructure (in io_account_mem() in io_uring/rsrc.c) when userspace
registers the buffers in io-uring.

---

There's no technical reason it can't but it keeps things simpler (with
the buffer pool, userspace can more optimally register the bufferpool
into one fixed slot since the buffers are contiguous), reduces the
surface area of configurations that need to be tested, and allows the
feature to be advertised under the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL capability flag
during init instead of needing a separate flag. I think in practice,
servers that would want the benefits from registered buffers would
also want the benefits from buffer pools, so I don't think we're
leaving out a wanted use case.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for zero-copy operations over io-uring in the fuse file system. It eliminates memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server by allowing the server to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The patch introduces a new feature that requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS capabilities, which enables the server to register relevant pages into its io-uring registered buffer table. This allows for more efficient read/write operations by reducing memory copies. The patch also includes documentation of the request flow for zero-copy write path.

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: reviewer questioned how FIXED_READ works in userspace and asked for clarification on its interaction with zero-copy io-uring
sentiment_signals: lack of clear technical concern or suggestion
review_comments.raw_body:
FIXED_READ takes an address, right?  How does all this work at the
userspace level?

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the FIXED_READ call in userspace, explaining that it takes a buf_index instead of an address and clarifying how the zero_copy_index is used to register pages for zero-copy operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
The userspace FIXED_READ call (eg set up usually through the liburing
api io_uring_prep_read_fixed() [1]) takes a buf_index, not an address.
Userspace would pass the ent->zero_copy_index as the buf index.
ent->zero_copy_index corresponds to the fixed buffer where userspace
told the kernel (at sqe registration time through the "uint16_t
zero_copy_index" init param) to place the zero-copied pages for that
sqe.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/io_uring_prep_read_fixed.3.html

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that the fixed operations in io-uring require both a buffer index and an address to determine the offset within the registered buffer, which is not currently provided.

Reviewer noted that passing a NULL address to the fixed op in io-uring operations for zero-copy buffers results in an offset of zero, and questioned whether this is correct behavior.
review_comments.raw_body:
AFAICS buf_index is required, but is not sufficient.  The fixed ops
require an address as well (to figure out the offset within the
registered buffer).

---

Okay so there are two buffers per sqe, one which is corresponds to a
real userspace address, and a zero copy one that is (by definition)
kernel-only and doesn't actually have an address.

Passing a NULL address to the fixed op seems okay in this case, but
that means the offset within the buffer is always zero.

Or am I misunderstanding something?

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the calculation of the offset into the fixed buffer for zero-copied registered buffers, explaining that io-uring internally sets the buffer's uaddr as 0 and the liburing API computes the offset by subtracting imu->ubuf from the provided address. The author did not indicate any plans to change their code.
review_comments.raw_body:
For zero-copied registered buffers, the "address" you pass in is the
raw offset into the buffer (eg if you want to read into the
zero-copied buffer starting at offset 4k in the buffer, you would call
io_uring_prep_read_fixed(sqe, fd, (void *)4096, len, file_off,
buf_index);).

On the kernel side, fuse uring calls io_buffer_register_bvec() to
place the application pages or page cache pages into the slot for the
registered buffer [1]. io-uring internally sets that buffer's uaddr as
0 (imu->ubuf = 0 in io_kernel_buffer_init() [2]) since the pages are
supplied kernel-side. The liburing io_uring_prep_read_fixed() api will
call into the kernel io_read_fixed() -> io_init_rw_fixed() ->
io_import_reg_buf() -> io_import_fixed(). In io_import_fixed(), the
offset into the fixed buffer is computed with "offset = buf_addr -
imu->ubuf" [3] where buf_addr is the u64 representation of the "void
*addr" that was passed into io_uring_prep_read_fixed().

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260612184840.4058966-4-joannelkoong@gmail.com/
[2] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260612184840.4058966-3-joannelkoong@gmail.com/
[3] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.1/source/io_uring/rsrc.c#L1072

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Miklos Szeredi requested a variant of the _fixed API for io-uring that takes an offset instead of an address, which would simplify handling for plain registered buffers and eliminate the need to look up addresses in cqe.
review_comments.raw_body:
Okay.  Could we have a variant of the _fixed API that takes an offset
instead of an address?

That would work much better for the plain registered buffers too, as
the fuse server wouldn't have to look up the address belonging to a
particular buf_index, just supply the offset that was returned in cqe
(as you suggested) to the _fixed op.

Hmm?

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author agrees that adding a variant of the _fixed API for zero-copy operations is a good idea, and plans to implement it

The author addressed a concern about the difference in address handling between zero-copied and non-zero-copied registered buffers, explaining that for non-zero-copied buffers, the fuse server would use the regular io_uring_prep_read_fixed() API and pass in the starting address plus offset.
review_comments.raw_body:
This is a liburing API [1]. We could add a variant of the _fixed API
for this (will need to come up with a good name for it...). Internally
it would still have to set sqe->addr = offset, as in [2], but I think
this is a good idea to make it less confusing.

---

For plain (non-zero-copied) registered buffers, the fuse server would
still need to pass in the starting address of the buffer pool, so I
think it would just use the regular io_uring_prep_read_fixed() API and
pass in starting_address + offset. It's only for buffers that are
zero-copied (eg pages supplied by the kernel) that the addr is the
offset; for non-zero-copied buffers, the addr must be an actual uaddr.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://github.com/axboe/liburing/blob/650d8fb7acb5952a12530b617f09da75b95c979c/src/include/liburing.h#L673
[2] https://github.com/axboe/liburing/blob/650d8fb7acb5952a12530b617f09da75b95c979c/src/include/liburing.h#L604

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: reviewer suggested adding a flag to the _FIXED io-uring op to indicate that the offset is being supplied in place of the address, allowing unconditional passing of the offset
review_comments.raw_body:
Why?  The kernel couldn't care less about the userspace address at
that point, it's literally just using the stored and supplied uaddr's
to calculate the offset into the buffer.

Adding a flag to the _FIXED op to indicate that the offset is being
supplied in place of the address should allow unconditionally passing
the offset to these ops.

Thanks,
Miklos

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that zero-copy over io-uring requires additional support in the kernel uapi, agreeing it's not currently implemented as described.

Author addressed a concern about liburing API consistency by explaining that the existing kernel IORING_OP_READ_FIXED op already interprets 'addr' as an offset for pages, and believes it would be consistent to apply this logic to plain registered buffers too.
review_comments.raw_body:
Agreed, but I don't think this is how it's currently supported on the
io-uring side in the kernel. There would need to be added support for
this in the kernel uapi.

---

Oh, I thought the issue from your first message about the liburing api
was that offset gets passed in via the "address" arg? For the existing
kernel IORING_OP_READ_FIXED op, "addr" is already interpreted as an
"offset" for pages that were kernel-supplied into the registered
buffer, so I don't think it'd be inconsistent to also allow plain
registered buffers to do that too.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 10/10] docs: fuse: add io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy techniques. The new documentation explains how buffer pools decouple the relationship between entries and payload buffers, allowing for more efficient memory usage and enabling optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. It also describes how to use this feature by setting a flag during registration and providing the payload buffer pool through an iovec payload in the first submission queue element (sqe). The documentation includes a high-level overview of how fuse manages the buffer pool internally, including how buffers are selected, copied, and recycled.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer pools and zero-copy to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing servers to pass in a managed buffer pool that reduces memory usage requirements and enables future optimizations. The decoupling of ents and buffers also eliminates the need for dedicated payload buffers, reducing memory waste. Additionally, the series introduces zero-copy functionality, which allows direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and registered buffers.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author simplified the logic for fetching and sending off the next request, removing duplicated code from fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring(), and decoupling request fetching from the send operation.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the tight coupling between ents and buffers in fuse io-uring, explaining that they have decoupled them by allowing servers to pass in a buffer pool (a contiguous allocation of buffers) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec payload in the first sqe. Buffer pool usage is set on a per-ring basis, and the kernel manages buffer selection and recycling through a buffer ring data structure.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the complexity of using registered buffers by explaining that they require setting an init flag and following standard protocol for using registered buffers, which is advertised to userspace via the FUSE_HAS_IO_URING_BUFPOOL init flag. The author provided benchmark results showing around a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads and a 35% improvement for zero-copy.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the complexity of the zero-copy implementation, explaining that it is necessary to handle both page-backed and non-page-backed arguments in a single request flow. The author provided detailed diagrams of the request flow for both write and read paths, highlighting how pages are registered and unregistered using io_buffer_register_bvec() and io_buffer_unregister(). The author also clarified that some requests may have both page-backed and non-page-backed arguments, which are handled separately.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Miklos Szeredi's feedback about the buffer pool interface being inconsistent and suggested a new command op (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) to pass in queue-level settings, including the number of reduced queues and bufpool. The author also explained that returning the buffer address directly would require 8 bytes, but using buf_id takes up only 2 bytes and allows for easier incremental buffer consumption in the future.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 5:06 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > Allow servers to use an io-uring registered buffer as the buffer pool.
> > When the server registers buffers with io-uring, the pages backing the
> > buffers are pinned upfront. This eliminates the overhead of
> > pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses per i/o
> > request. This also allows servers to use the same registered buffers for
> > subsequent backing store I/O (eg read_fixed/write_fixed), keeping data
> > in the same pinned pages without additional pinning or mapping overhead
> > required.
>
> Are pinned pages accounted and limited?

Yes, pinned pages are accounted and limited by the kernel io-uring
infrastructure (in io_account_mem() in io_uring/rsrc.c) when userspace
registers the buffers in io-uring.

>
> > Registered buffers require the buffer pool (FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL) to be
> > enabled.
>
> Is there any particular reason for this?  Not saying this is bad, just
> wondering...

There's no technical reason it can't but it keeps things simpler (with
the buffer pool, userspace can more optimally register the bufferpool
into one fixed slot since the buffers are contiguous), reduces the
surface area of configurations that need to be tested, and allows the
feature to be advertised under the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL capability flag
during init instead of needing a separate flag. I think in practice,
servers that would want the benefits from registered buffers would
also want the benefits from buffer pools, so I don't think we're
leaving out a wanted use case.

Thanks,
Joanne

---

On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> > registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> > payload in the first sqe.
>
> I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
> the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?

Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
(FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in. This would have the
nice side effect of doing the queue creation in this op instead of
right now creating the queue lazily when the first sqe gets
registered. On the userspace side, this adds a bit more friction (eg a
two-phase setup where userspace submits the queue setup cmd, reaps its
completion, and reuses that sqe to register an entry), but that seems
like a reasonable tradeoff.

Does this sound ok to you and Bernd?

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/fuse-devel/20260529-reduced-nr-ring-queues_3-v5-0-1dc08c2fccf6@bsbernd.com/T/#u

>
> Thanks,
> Miklos

On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 5:33 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > Buffer ring request flow
> > ------------------------
> > |  Kernel                                  |  FUSE daemon
> > |                                          |
> > |  [client request arrives]                |
> > |  >fuse_uring_send()                      |
> > |    [select payload buf from pool]        |
> > |    >fuse_uring_select_buffer()           |
> > |    [copy headers to ent]                 |
> > |    >copy_header_to_ring()                |
> > |    [copy payload to selected buf]        |
> > |    >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()            |
> > |    [set buf_id in ent_in_out header]     |
> > |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                  |
> > |                                          |  [CQE received]
> > |                                          |  [read headers]
> > |                                          |  [read payload from buf_id]
>
> How does buf_id map to a buffer address?

The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.

>
> Wouldn't it be simpler to provide the buffer address directly?

This would have to be returned as part of the struct
fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
use. Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.

>
> We also need some uAPI documentation (generally not just for this
> patchset).  What about a new directory:
> Documentation/filesystems/fuse/uapi?

That sounds good. I'll write some uAPI documentation for this and add
it to that new directory.

Thanks,
Joanne

---

On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 6:05 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>

> > |                                           |

> > |          [register pages at
> >                  ent->zero_copy_index]      |
> > |        [ent->zero_copied = true]          |
> > |                                           |
> > |      >fuse_copy_args()                    |
> > |        [skip_folio_copy => return 0       |
> > |         for page arg, skip data copy]     |
> > |                                           |
> > |    >copy_header_to_ring(RING_ENT)         |
> > |      [memcpy ent_in_out]                  |
> > |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                   |
> > |                                           |
> > |                                           | [CQE received]
> > |                                           |
> > |                                           | [issue io_uring READ at
> > |                                           |  ent->zero_copy_index]
>
> FIXED_READ takes an address, right?  How does all this work at the
> userspace level?

The userspace FIXED_READ call (eg set up usually through the liburing
api io_uring_prep_read_fixed() [1]) takes a buf_index, not an address.
Userspace would pass the ent->zero_copy_index as the buf index.
ent->zero_copy_index corresponds to the fixed buffer where userspace
told the kernel (at sqe registration time through the "uint16_t
zero_copy_index" init param) to place the zero-copied pages for that
sqe.

Thanks,
Joanne

[1] https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man3/io_uring_prep_read_fixed.3.html

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed concerns about interface consistency and queue management in the fuse io-uring buffer pools patch series. She acknowledged that decoupling the buffer(s) from the sqe's means registration should be decoupled as well, but disagreed with Miklos' suggestion to add a separate op for bufpool registration. Instead, she proposed using the request type to dispatch metadata ops to non-io queues and read/write ops to io queues, which would simplify queue selection logic.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Jun 17, 2026 at 7:22 AM Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com> wrote:
>
> On 6/17/26 14:49, Joanne Koong wrote:
> > On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 6:26 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
> >>
> >> On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 at 07:28, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >>>
> >>> On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
> >>>>
> >>>> On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >>>>
> >>>>> To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> >>>>> registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> >>>>> payload in the first sqe.
> >>>>
> >>>> I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
> >>>> the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?
> >>>
> >>> Looking at Bernd's reduced queue series [1], I think we'd most likely
> >>> want to also pass in the number of reduced queues separate from sqe
> >>> registration. I think along the lines of the config sqe Bernd brought
> >>> up, it seems cleanest to add a new cmd op
> >>> (FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_QUEUE_SETUP) where queue-level settings (eg flags,
> >>> number of queues, bufpool, etc) gets passed in.
> >>
> >> Why not a separate op for bufpool registration, so it can be done
> >> independently of the setup, possibly at some later time?
> >
> > I'm not really convinced there's a compelling use case for needing
> > multiple bufpools in one queue. This was the discussion with Bernd in
> > [1] about this. In my opinion, incremental buffer consumption and
> > allowing different queues to support different payload sizes would
> > address the use cases of multiple bufpools within one queue, but would
> > be simpler.
>
> To be honest, I have no idea how to implement queues with different IO
> sizes. What would be the logic to select a queue? And what is if we

With the separation between non-IO queues and IO-queues, I think we
could just look at the request type, and dispatch accordingly so that
the metadata ops go to the non-io queues and the read/write ops go to
the io queues. I think the dispatch logic would be otherwise the same
for choosing which particular queue within the non-io or io queues.

> start with an single max-IO buf and a single queue (per numa) and then
> scale up based on need? Let's say we have:
>
> Start 1 queue (per numa maybe), one max payload buf in that queue and
> allow to scale up to max nr CPUs for the cpu to queue-id mapping. But
> now additionally we get for the same CPU
>
> - multiple queues of the same buf size
> - multiple queues of different buf sizes
>

I'm not seeing why this would be an issue. For multiple queues of the
same buffer size, it would just be normal queue selection and for
different buffer sizes, we'd jjust have two pools of queues (non-io
and io) and select based on request type first which pool then apply
normal queue selection within that pool. I think if a server has needs
for multiple categories of buffer sizes beyond just non-io vs io,
having multiple bufpools per queue makes more sense than dedicated
queues to the specific categories of the different buffer sizes, but I
doubt this level of granularity would be needed.

>
> Also, every queue has its own ring, syscall aggregation is not given
> anymore.

Syscall aggregation still applies within each ring, but I don't think
sysclal aggregation is really a big factor given that libfuse and the
server handles requests as they arrive and respond as soon as the
request is handled, instead of batching multiple responses before
submitting; on the dispatch side, requests are sent one by one as they
come in rather than batched, so there's no aggregation to lose.

Thanks,
Joanne

---

On Thu, Jun 18, 2026 at 12:52 AM Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com> wrote:
>
>
>
> On 6/18/26 09:32, Joanne Koong wrote:
> > On Wed, Jun 17, 2026 at 7:22 AM Bernd Schubert <bernd@bsbernd.com> wrote:
> >>
> >> On 6/17/26 14:49, Joanne Koong wrote:
> >>> On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 6:26 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
> >>>>
> >>>> On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 at 07:28, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >>>>>
> >>>>> On Mon, Jun 15, 2026 at 4:41 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
> >>>>>>

> >>> be simpler.
> >>
> >> To be honest, I have no idea how to implement queues with different IO
> >> sizes. What would be the logic to select a queue? And what is if we
> >
> > With the separation between non-IO queues and IO-queues, I think we
> > could just look at the request type, and dispatch accordingly so that
> > the metadata ops go to the non-io queues and the read/write ops go to
> > the io queues. I think the dispatch logic would be otherwise the same
> > for choosing which particular queue within the non-io or io queues.
> >
> >> start with an single max-IO buf and a single queue (per numa) and then
> >> scale up based on need? Let's say we have:
> >>
> >> Start 1 queue (per numa maybe), one max payload buf in that queue and
> >> allow to scale up to max nr CPUs for the cpu to queue-id mapping. But
> >> now additionally we get for the same CPU
> >>
> >> - multiple queues of the same buf size
> >> - multiple queues of different buf sizes
> >>
> >
> > I'm not seeing why this would be an issue. For multiple queues of the
> > same buffer size, it would just be normal queue selection and for
> > different buffer sizes, we'd jjust have two pools of queues (non-io
> > and io) and select based on request type first which pool then apply
> > normal queue selection within that pool. I think if a server has needs
> > for multiple categories of buffer sizes beyond just non-io vs io,
> > having multiple bufpools per queue makes more sense than dedicated
> > queues to the specific categories of the different buffer sizes, but I
> > doubt this level of granularity would be needed.
>
> I'm pretty much lost. It is a 3 dimensional matrix, made of cpus,
> buffer-size and buffers. I really want to see a logic how you figured
> which queue has free buffers.

We already have logic for this (done in your reduced queue series). I
think we could just reuse that logic. A basic implementation would be
having a separate bitmap for non-IO queues vs IO queues, and then once
you figure out if the request is an IO or non-IO request, the rest of
the dispatch logic would be the same for choosing a queue from either
the non-IO queue pool or the IO queue pool. If you're concerned about
dispatch complexity, I think separate bufpools per queue ends up being
more complicated.

>
> >
> >>
> >> Also, every queue has its own ring, syscall aggregation is not given
> >> anymore.
> >
> > Syscall aggregation still applies within each ring, but I don't think
> > sysclal aggregation is really a big factor given that libfuse and the
> > server handles requests as they arrive and respond as soon as the
> > request is handled, instead of batching multiple responses before
> > submitting; on the dispatch side, requests are sent one by one as they
> > come in rather than batched, so there's no aggregation to lose.
>
> Depends, if fuse server starts with 1 buffer per queue and slowly scales
> up, you basically get exactly one buffer per queue. That is the end of
> aggregation.
>

I really don't think servers will start with 1 buffer per queue.

Happy to discuss this together in the call later today.

Thanks,
Joanne

On Thu, Jun 18, 2026 at 1:37 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
>
> On Thu, 18 Jun 2026 at 09:03, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> > imo the main benefit is for incremental buffer consumption (allowing
> > non-overlapping parts of a buffer to be used by multiple entries
> > concurrently). To recycle back a buffer that is used by multiple
> > entries, it would require all requests to have been completed but I
> > don't think in practice this is a bottleneck with reasonably sized
> > buffer pools across queues. imo incremental buffer consumption scales
> > more dynamically with runtime workloads than preallocating fixed
> > buffers of different sizes across different queues trying to
> > anticipate / guess workload needs. Overprovisioning small buffers
> > wastes memory if those requests never appear, while splitting the
> > memory budget towards smaller pools reduces capacity for concurrent
> > large requests, and workload patterns might need either at different
> > times.
>
> Hmm, so why partition this memory into buffers up front?
>
> We could just as well say that managing that chunk of  memory is up to
> the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
> redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?

That's a good point, especially since we can just return back offset
into the bufpools instead of buffer id. That's a much cleaner
interface. Though I guess if we want to be forwards-compatible with
having the option of multiple bufpools per queue in the future, we'd
have to add bufsize to the uapi here now. Do you think we should just
keep it as is then?

>
> > I think we should still make the design for bufpools
> > forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
> > comes up in the future,
>
> Yeah, and separating buffer registration into a separate op would be
> one step in that direction.   It doesn't have to support multiple
> bufpools from the start, but splitting out separate functions during
> initialization is a good idea regardless.

I'm not sure I agree that splitting it out would be a good idea if we
don't ever end up supporting multiple bufpools (imo, having this in
QUEUE_SETUP would logically make sense and be more ergonomic) but I'm
happy to make this change.

>
> > From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
> > different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
> > requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
> > requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests.
>
> Note, "queue" could mean two things here:
>
>  - fuse's waiting list
>  - cqe's on the ring
>
> Currently there's 1:1 relation between those, but that's not mandatory
> going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
> types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.

Even with separate waiting queues and the kernel prioritizing sending
metadata requests before io, isn't there the issue that if the
requests/entries on the ring are all IO requests and then a storm of
metadata ops come in, they're still backed up waiting for the IO
requests to be finished? Or am I missing something?

On the userspace side, I think they'd need a threadpool which would
add extra context-switching overhead and would have to reserve a few
threads in that pool just for non-IO requests, and then have a way to
inspect requests in the ring to prioritize the non-IO ones, which
seems nontrivial. But maybe there's some simpler way to do this that
I'm not seeing.

Thanks,
Joanne

---

On Thu, Jun 18, 2026 at 10:20 AM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> On Thu, Jun 18, 2026 at 1:37 AM Miklos Szeredi <miklos@szeredi.hu> wrote:
> >
> > On Thu, 18 Jun 2026 at 09:03, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
> >
> > > imo the main benefit is for incremental buffer consumption (allowing
> > > non-overlapping parts of a buffer to be used by multiple entries
> > > concurrently). To recycle back a buffer that is used by multiple
> > > entries, it would require all requests to have been completed but I
> > > don't think in practice this is a bottleneck with reasonably sized
> > > buffer pools across queues. imo incremental buffer consumption scales
> > > more dynamically with runtime workloads than preallocating fixed
> > > buffers of different sizes across different queues trying to
> > > anticipate / guess workload needs. Overprovisioning small buffers
> > > wastes memory if those requests never appear, while splitting the
> > > memory budget towards smaller pools reduces capacity for concurrent
> > > large requests, and workload patterns might need either at different
> > > times.
> >
> > Hmm, so why partition this memory into buffers up front?
> >
> > We could just as well say that managing that chunk of  memory is up to
> > the kernel.  This would simplify the interface: the buffer size is
> > redundant, since it's the same as the max I/O size, no?
>
> That's a good point, especially since we can just return back offset
> into the bufpools instead of buffer id. That's a much cleaner
> interface. Though I guess if we want to be forwards-compatible with
> having the option of multiple bufpools per queue in the future, we'd
> have to add bufsize to the uapi here now. Do you think we should just
> keep it as is then?

Ignore the last two sentences please, I didn't think through your
original message.

I like your idea a lot about just passing a chunk of memory to the
kernel and offloading the management logic entirely to the kernel.
Maybe I'm missing something obvious, but why would multiple pools per
queue still be needed? If the server needs more capacity, why not just
have the server spin up a new queue? That would have the included
benefit of adding more concurrency, and dynamically adding more queues
is a feature that would be added to fuse uring regardless. For
multipools in one queue, the pools would be discontiguous anyways,
which wouldn't help with fragmentation. I don't think adding a queue
incurs that much memory or cpu overhead, and imo the scenarios where a
server would want to just add more capacity but not parallelism are
uncommon.

>
> >
> > > I think we should still make the design for bufpools
> > > forward-compatible with supporting multiple bufpools if a use case
> > > comes up in the future,
> >
> > Yeah, and separating buffer registration into a separate op would be
> > one step in that direction.   It doesn't have to support multiple
> > bufpools from the start, but splitting out separate functions during
> > initialization is a good idea regardless.
>
> I'm not sure I agree that splitting it out would be a good idea if we
> don't ever end up supporting multiple bufpools (imo, having this in
> QUEUE_SETUP would logically make sense and be more ergonomic) but I'm
> happy to make this change.
>
> >
> > > From my perspective, the use case I see for queues supporting
> > > different payload sizes is for separating IO requests from non-IO
> > > requests (eg lookups, getattrs) onto different queues so that non-IO
> > > requests don't get stuck waiting behind larger IO requests.
> >
> > Note, "queue" could mean two things here:
> >
> >  - fuse's waiting list
> >  - cqe's on the ring
> >
> > Currently there's 1:1 relation between those, but that's not mandatory
> > going forward.  So we could have multiple waiting queues for different
> > types of requests, but a single uring for sending them to the server.
>
> Even with separate waiting queues and the kernel prioritizing sending
> metadata requests before io, isn't there the issue that if the
> requests/entries on the ring are all IO requests and then a storm of
> metadata ops come in, they're still backed up waiting for the IO
> requests to be finished? Or am I missing something?

Thinking about this some more, I think the kernel could just
pre-reserve some ring entries specifically for metadata ops / non-IO
requests. That solves it on the kernel side but the userspace side
would still need a threadpool of some sort if its backend IO blocks
its queue thread.

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Jens Axboe
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Jens Axboe noted that patches 1-6, which are refactoring changes, have been out since October and have value on their own, independent of the bufpool and zero-copy work, and requested that they be picked up for 7.2 so they don't have to be carried and repeatedly rebased.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 6/12/26 3:46 PM, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Fri, Jun 12, 2026 at 2:06?PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>> This series adds buffer pools (implemented internally as a buffer ring) and
>> zero-copy to fuse io-uring.
>>
>> Prior to this series, ents and buffers are tightly coupled where each entry
>> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
>> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
>> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
>> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
>>
>> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
>> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
>> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
>> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
>> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
>>
>> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
>> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
>> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
>>
>> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
>> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
>> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
>> registered buffers. Zero copy (patch 9) has a dependency on io-uring registered
>> bvec changes in [1].
> 
> Miklos,
> 
> The zero-copy patch has a dependency on the bvec patches in Jens's
> io-uring tree. Since the merge window opens Monday, it's probably too
> tight now to cross-coordinate this for 7.2. What do you prefer for
> syncing between the two trees? If Jens stages the patches in a stable
> branch, are you fine with pulling that in and then applying the fuse
> patches on top?
> 
> Patches 7 and 8 (bufpools and registered buffers) don't have this
> dependency, but to make the uapi cleaner under one single umbrella
> flag, it would be better to co-ship them with the zero-copy patch.
> 
> Patches 1-6 are refactoring changes that have been out since October
> and have value on their own, independent of the bufpool and zero-copy
> work. Could you please pick these up for 7.2 so they don't have to be
> carried and repeatedly rebased?

FWIW, I reviewed the patches again and they still look fine to me. So
the 4 io_uring prep patches are staged, they are in my
for-7.2/io_uring-fuse branch. They can either go in for this coming
merge window, or we can carry them forward if fuse isn't quite ready
yet.

Since there will be conflicts with either the io_uring branches or block
branches, the above branch is for-7.2/io_uring + for-7.2/block to avoid
that.

-- 
Jens Axboe

review_comments.author: Miklos Szeredi
review_comments.summary: Miklos Szeredi pointed out that the buffer pool interface is inconsistent, as it decouples buffers from sqe's but not registration. He suggested providing the buffer address directly instead of using buf_id and requested uAPI documentation.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:47, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> Patches 1-6 are refactoring changes that have been out since October
> and have value on their own, independent of the bufpool and zero-copy
> work. Could you please pick these up for 7.2 so they don't have to be
> carried and repeatedly rebased?

Applied these.

Thanks,
Miklos

---

On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> To use this, the server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during
> registration and provide the payload buffer pool through the iovec
> payload in the first sqe.

I find this interface inconsistent.   Decoupling the buffer(s) from
the seq's means that the registration should be decoupled as well, no?

Thanks,
Miklos

---

On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> Allow servers to use an io-uring registered buffer as the buffer pool.
> When the server registers buffers with io-uring, the pages backing the
> buffers are pinned upfront. This eliminates the overhead of
> pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses per i/o
> request. This also allows servers to use the same registered buffers for
> subsequent backing store I/O (eg read_fixed/write_fixed), keeping data
> in the same pinned pages without additional pinning or mapping overhead
> required.

Are pinned pages accounted and limited?

> Registered buffers require the buffer pool (FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL) to be
> enabled.

Is there any particular reason for this?  Not saying this is bad, just
wondering...

Thanks,
Miklos

---

On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> Buffer ring request flow
> ------------------------
> |  Kernel                                  |  FUSE daemon
> |                                          |
> |  [client request arrives]                |
> |  >fuse_uring_send()                      |
> |    [select payload buf from pool]        |
> |    >fuse_uring_select_buffer()           |
> |    [copy headers to ent]                 |
> |    >copy_header_to_ring()                |
> |    [copy payload to selected buf]        |
> |    >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()            |
> |    [set buf_id in ent_in_out header]     |
> |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                  |
> |                                          |  [CQE received]
> |                                          |  [read headers]
> |                                          |  [read payload from buf_id]

How does buf_id map to a buffer address?

Wouldn't it be simpler to provide the buffer address directly?

We also need some uAPI documentation (generally not just for this
patchset).  What about a new directory:
Documentation/filesystems/fuse/uapi?

Thanks,
Milklos

---

On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:07, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> The request flow for the zero-copy write path (client writes data,
> server reads it) is as follows:
> =======================================================================
> |  Kernel                                   |  FUSE server
> |                                           |
> |  "write(fd, buf, 1MB)"                    |
> |                                           |
> |  >sys_write()                             |
> |    >fuse_file_write_iter()                |
> |      >fuse_send_one()                     |
> |        [req->args->in_pages = true]       |
> |        [folios hold client write data]    |
> |                                           |
> |  >fuse_uring_copy_to_ring()               |
> |    >copy_header_to_ring(IN_OUT)           |
> |      [memcpy fuse_in_header]              |
> |    >copy_header_to_ring(OP)               |
> |      [memcpy write_in header]             |
> |                                           |
> |    >fuse_uring_args_to_ring()             |
> |      >setup_fuse_copy_state()             |
> |        [skip_folio_copy = true]           |
> |                                           |
> |      >fuse_uring_set_up_zero_copy()       |
> |        [folio_get for each client folio]  |
> |        [build bio_vec array from folios]  |
> |        >io_buffer_register_bvec()         |
> |          [register pages at
>                  ent->zero_copy_index]      |
> |        [ent->zero_copied = true]          |
> |                                           |
> |      >fuse_copy_args()                    |
> |        [skip_folio_copy => return 0       |
> |         for page arg, skip data copy]     |
> |                                           |
> |    >copy_header_to_ring(RING_ENT)         |
> |      [memcpy ent_in_out]                  |
> |    >io_uring_cmd_done()                   |
> |                                           |
> |                                           | [CQE received]
> |                                           |
> |                                           | [issue io_uring READ at
> |                                           |  ent->zero_copy_index]

FIXED_READ takes an address, right?  How does all this work at the
userspace level?

Thanks,
Miklos

---

On Fri, 12 Jun 2026 at 23:06, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> This series adds buffer pools (implemented internally as a buffer ring) and
> zero-copy to fuse io-uring.
>
> Prior to this series, ents and buffers are tightly coupled where each entry
> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
>
> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
>
> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
>
> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
> registered buffers. Zero copy (patch 9) has a dependency on io-uring registered
> bvec changes in [1].

Overall I think this series is good, the only part notably lacking is
concise uAPI documentation.

I'd even suggest dropping all internal implementation details from the
documentation that was provided, since adding those details to the
commit messages (which you did) should be sufficient.

Thanks,
Miklos


>
> This series is on top of commit f21c8b1a3b740 in for-next.
>
> The throughput improvements from registered buffers and zero-copy depends on
> how much of the server's per-request latency is spent on data copying vs
> backing I/O. When backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible
> fraction of overall latency. Please also note that for the server to
> read/write into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
> as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
> server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
> overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
> from eliminating the memcpy.
>
> In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
> system, using registered buffers showed around a 10% throughput improvement
> for direct randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2400 MiB/s), a 4% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2620 MiB/s), a 8% improvement for buffered
> randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2280 MiB/s), and a 6% improvement for buffered
> sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2670 MiB/s).
>
> Zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
> randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
> buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
> for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s). I didn't see
> enough of a clear improvement for writes due to write latency being I/O
> dominated.
>
> The benchmarks were run using:
>   fio --name=test_run --ioengine=sync --rw=rand{read,write} --bs=1M
>   --size=1G --numjobs=2 --ramp_time=30 --group_reporting=1
>
> The libfuse changes can be found in [2]. To test the server, run:
> sudo ~/libfuse/build/example/passthrough_hp ~/src ~/mounts/tmp
> --nopassthrough -o io_uring_zero_copy -o io_uring_q_depth=8
> Once this series is merged, the libfuse changes will be tidied up and
> submitted upstream.
>
> Thanks,
> Joanne
>
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260612184840.4058966-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#t
> [2] https://github.com/joannekoong/libfuse/commits/zero_copy_v4/

On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 at 07:46, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> The userspace FIXED_READ call (eg set up usually through the liburing
> api io_uring_prep_read_fixed() [1]) takes a buf_index, not an address.

AFAICS buf_index is required, but is not sufficient.  The fixed ops
require an address as well (to figure out the offset within the
registered buffer).

> Userspace would pass the ent->zero_copy_index as the buf index.
> ent->zero_copy_index corresponds to the fixed buffer where userspace
> told the kernel (at sqe registration time through the "uint16_t
> zero_copy_index" init param) to place the zero-copied pages for that
> sqe.

Okay so there are two buffers per sqe, one which is corresponds to a
real userspace address, and a zero copy one that is (by definition)
kernel-only and doesn't actually have an address.

Passing a NULL address to the fixed op seems okay in this case, but
that means the offset within the buffer is always zero.

Or am I misunderstanding something?

Thanks,
Miklos

On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 at 07:38, Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:

> The server knows the starting address of the bufpool (since it
> allocated it) and it knows the size of each buffer in the pool. It can
> compute from the buf_id the address corresponding to the buffer.

But which buffer?   The interface is supposed to handle multiple
pools, in which case the server needs to find the pool and the buffer
within the pool.

FIXED ops also require buf_index.  Things are getting complicated...

> This would have to be returned as part of the struct
> fuse_uring_ent_in_out. There's currently 12 bytes that are free to
> use.

Does the 12 bytes limit come from the cqe?

> Returning buf_id takes up 2 bytes and I think is better than
> using 8 bytes to provide the buffer address. However, returning the
> buf id makes adding incremental buffer consumption in the future a bit
> trickier (eg the server would need to keep track of the offset into
> the previously used buffer and do any alignment rounding up). Maybe a
> good compromise is returning a uint32_t offset that corresponds to the
> offset in the bufpool? The buf size is a uint32_t, so I think this
> would work just as well as providing the buffer address directly.

Yeah, offset sounds good, but still need to select between the pools.
So should that be the "pool index" that would be the same as
"buf_index" for registered buffers?

Thanks,
Miklos

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: use existing highatomic reserves on the buddy fastpath

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/page_alloc: use existing highatomic reserves on the buddy fastpath
[PATCH v2] mm/page_alloc: use existing highatomic reserves on the buddy fastpath

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's page allocation mechanism to allow high-priority non-blocking allocations to use existing MIGRATE_HIGHATOMIC reserves on the buddy fastpath, rather than falling back to other migratetypes. The ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC flag is updated to only provide access to these reserves without permission to grow them, and a new flag ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC_RESERVE is introduced to specifically allow growing the reserves. This change improves performance in certain workloads by reducing the number of highatomic misses.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer suggested replacing the new alloc flag ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC_RESERVE with a check for ALLOC_WMARK_MIN to determine when to reserve high-atomic pageblocks, eliminating the need for another flag.

The reviewer noted that the patch introduces a mismatch between the fastpath and slowpath allocation flags, specifically allowing highatomic reserves to be tapped for costly orders in the slowpath but not in the fastpath.

Reviewer Johannes Weiner noted that the patch introduces duplicated logic in gfp_to_alloc_flags(), suggesting a more robust approach by providing an alternative implementation

The reviewer suggested optimizing the code by reusing the existing function gfp_to_alloc_flags_nonblocking() instead of calling gfp_to_alloc_flags() and then applying a bitwise AND operation.
review_comments.raw_body:
You could check ALLOC_WMARK_MIN to determine the slowpath. This way
you wouldn't need another alloc flag:

	/* Slowpath (precarious) high-atomic allocation. Maybe reserve block */
	if (unlikely((alloc_flags & (ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC|ALLOC_WMARK_MIN)) == (ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC|ALLOC_WMARK_MIN)))
		reserve_highatomic_pageblock(page, order, zone);

[ we really ought to generalize gfp_has_flags() ... ]

---

There seems to be a mismatch between this and gfp_to_alloc_flags()
(slowpath), where slowpath is still allowed to tap highatomic reserves
for costly orders. Is that on purpose?

---

This duplicates gfp_to_alloc_flags() logic which seems fragile. How
about the below:

---

alloc_flags |= gfp_to_alloc_flags(gfp, order) & ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC;

Or factor gfp_to_alloc_flags_nonblocking() from gfp_to_alloc_flags()
and reuse that here, to save a few cycles in the fast path.

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that using watermark low vs min to determine fast/slow path is a viable approach, but notes that watermark bits need to be masked out due to ALLOC_WMARK_MIN being zero.

Author agreed to remove the order cap from ALLOC_HIGHATOMIC_RESERVE in a future patch (v3) after reconsidering its impact on allocator outcomes for frequent net allocations.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hmm so we just use watermark low vs min to determine fast/slow path. We
would need to mask out the watermark bits since ALLOC_WMARK_MIN is zero,
but I think the idea can work.

---

Yes, the intention was to improve allocator outcomes for frequent net
allocations. So I kept the policy narrow. But I thought about this some
more and agree it's worth removing the order cap. With the cap, if a
costly+ order atomic allocation reaches the buddy path there may be two
non-ideal outcomes: fallback to some other migratetype in the fastpath
or enter the slowpath and make use of any available reserves. In the
latter case, the slowpath could have been avoided altogether. So I'll
make this change in v3.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/bpf_memcontrol: mark BPF memcg kfuncs static

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/bpf_memcontrol: mark BPF memcg kfuncs static

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch marks several BPF memory control functions in bpf_memcontrol.c as static, since they are only referenced by BPF programs and resolved through BTF (Binary Interface Format). This change is made to remove unnecessary external linkage. The affected functions include bpf_get_root_mem_cgroup, bpf_get_mem_cgroup, bpf_put_mem_cgroup, bpf_mem_cgroup_vm_events, and bpf_mem_cgroup_usage. These changes are based on the introduction of BPF kfuncs in previous commits (5904db9891f8, 5c7db3239c9f, and 99430ab8b804). The patch is reported to fix a kernel test robot issue and closes a related bug report.

review_comments.author: Alexei Starovoitov
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Alexei Starovoitov pointed out that marking BPF memcg kfuncs as static is incorrect, citing an example from net/ipv4/ where similar functions work by chance due to their addresses being taken, and stated that in general kfuncs cannot be declared static.
review_comments.raw_body:
sashiko disappoints. It couldn't notice that kfuncs should not be static.
We have few "__bpf_kfunc static" in net/ipv4/
that work by sort-of "luck", since their addresses are taken.

In general kfuncs cannot be declared static.

pw-bot: cr

review_comments.author: Roman Gushchin
review_comments.summary: The reviewer, Roman Gushchin, notes that the existing code and documentation imply that BPF kfuncs can be static, citing a dismissed concern from the logs about compiler optimization breaking BTF resolution.
review_comments.raw_body:
It seems like it thought hard on it but failed based on the existing
code and a bit vague documentation in Documentation/bpf/kfuncs.rst:

  kfunc definitions should also always be annotated with the ``__bpf_kfunc``
  macro. This prevents issues such as the compiler inlining the kfunc if it's a
  static kernel function, or the function ...

So it reads like bpf kfuncs can be static.

This is the reasoning from the logs (a dismissed concern, to be precise):
{
      "type": "Dead Code / Linker Error",

      "description": "Marking kfuncs as static could cause the compiler
      to optimize them away, breaking BPF verifier BTF resolution.",

      "reasoning": "The patch adds `static` to multiple `__bpf_kfunc`
      annotated functions that are not called anywhere in C
      code. Normally, a compiler would remove unused static functions or
      fail to expose them in object files, which could prevent the BTF
      ID generation logic (`resolve_btfids`) from finding them. However,
      the `__bpf_kfunc` macro expands to include `__used` and
      `__retain`, which strictly instructs the compiler and linker to
      preserve the functions in the object file regardless of
      usage. Additionally, `resolve_btfids` and `BTF_ID_FLAGS`
      successfully parse and resolve `STB_LOCAL` static symbols, making
      this change completely safe and correct.",

      "locations": [
        {
          "file": "mm/bpf_memcontrol.c",
          "function_or_symbol": null,
          "line_range": null,
          "why_this_location_matters": "This applies to all the
          functions modified by the patch."
        }
      ]
    }


Thanks

review_comments.author: Alexei Starovoitov
review_comments.summary: The reviewer, Alexei Starovoitov, pointed out that using 'static' instead of '__used' or '__retain' may lead to function renaming by the compiler in LTO mode, and suggested updating the documentation to address this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Almost, except it's not clear what takes priority for the compiler.
__bpf_kfunc used to have '__used' and it was enough until clang
got too aggressive in LTO mode. So we added '__retain' and so far
it seems to be holding, but sashiko is missing that both of these
attributes don't say anything about _name_ of the function.
'static' keyword means that compiler is free to rename it and
compilers do take advantage of that from time to time,
while __used and __retain don't add 'do-not-rename' restriction
to the compiler.

Also look at it from the other angle.. why anyone would add
'static' when the function is clearly not static to this particular
compilation unit. It will be used outside of it by bpf progs
that are obviuosly not part of this .c file.

Documentation/bpf/kfuncs.rst needs to be updated.

JP,
pls send a patch to update the docs and include few words
to ignore sparse.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort
[PATCH v3 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort
[PATCH v4 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to the Linux kernel on arm64 architecture to handle CPU wedging by escalating smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) as a last resort. When a CPU is wedged with interrupts masked, it ignores stop IPIs and without pseudo-NMI there's no NMI IPI to escalate to. The patch introduces a third rung in smp_send_stop() that signals an SDEI event 0 at the remaining online CPUs, which firmware delivers regardless of the target's DAIF (Debug Access Interface Flag). This allows the CPU to reach a wedged CPU and acknowledge by going offline. The stop bookkeeping is also folded into a shared function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop() for consistency.

review_comments.author: Doug Anderson
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Doug Anderson expressed concern that the SDEI NMI handler may not be able to handle CPUs that are in a wedged state, as it relies on firmware delivering the event regardless of the target's DAIF (Device Access Interrupt Flag), but this assumption may not hold for all firmware implementations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds support for delivering NMI-like events to interrupt-masked arm64 CPUs using the SDEI software-signalled event mechanism. The goal is to provide a more efficient way to deliver such events, avoiding the pseudo-NMI hot-path cost. The first user of this feature is arch_trigger_cpumask_backtrace(), which can now reach masked CPUs without falling back to an IPI path when SDEI is available. A stub implementation is provided for cases where SDEI is not present or disabled.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 2/4] firmware: arm_sdei: add SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL support

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] firmware: arm_sdei: add SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL support

[PATCH v4 2/4] firmware: arm_sdei: add SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL support

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called sdei_event_signal() to the arm_sdei firmware driver, which signals a software-signalled event (event 0) to another processor element (PE). The function is designed to be safe to call from non-maskable interrupt (NMI) or crash context and takes no locks. It uses the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call to deliver the event NMI-like even when interrupts are masked on the target PE. The patch also updates the include files to reflect the new function.

[PATCH v3 0/3] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI
[PATCH v4 0/4] arm64: cross-CPU NMI via SDEI

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture using SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure). This is necessary for certain debug and observability features, such as capturing CPU backtraces and crash dumps, which require interrupting a CPU that has its interrupts locally masked. The existing pseudo-NMI mechanism is too expensive in terms of performance overhead, so this new approach uses SDEI to deliver the NMI at a lower cost. This allows for more accurate debugging and observability features without introducing significant performance regressions.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3] sched/mmcid: fix OOB clear_bit when CID is MM_CID_UNSET in fixup path

version_history.subject: [PATCH] sched/mmcid: fix OOB clear_bit when CID is MM_CID_UNSET in fixup path
[PATCH v3] sched/mmcid: fix OOB clear_bit when CID is MM_CID_UNSET in fixup path

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a memory corruption issue in the Linux kernel's CID (Context ID) management system. When a task is running on a CPU and its CID is still unset, the clear_bit function can be called with an out-of-bounds bit number, leading to a deterministic memory corruption. The patch adds an additional check to prevent this issue by guarding against MM_CID_UNSET when setting the transition bit. This fix ensures that only genuine task-owned CIDs are handled correctly, preventing potential use-after-free errors and memory corruption.

review_comments.author: Mathieu Desnoyers
review_comments.summary: Reviewer asked Rik to verify if the MM_CID_UNSET issue is isolated to the reproduced bug or if there are other instances in the code.
sentiment_signals: lack of explicit approval or disapproval

Thomas, can you have a look as well in case we missed something
subtle ?

Rik, did you check whether there are other instances of that
MM_CID_UNSET issue lurking in the code, or was your analysis
focused on the reproduced bug ?

Reviewed-by: Mathieu Desnoyers <mathieu.desnoyers@efficios.com>

Thanks,

Mathieu

-- 
Mathieu Desnoyers
EfficiOS Inc.
https://www.efficios.com

review_comments.author: Thomas Gleixner
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the explanation provided by the original author made the issue clear, and expressed gratitude for their efforts in identifying and fixing the problem.
review_comments.raw_body:
Duh. Now that you explained it it's obvious. Thanks for tracking this
nasty down!

review_comments.author: tip-bot2 Riel
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the patch does not handle the case where a task's CID is MM_CID_UNSET, which can lead to an out-of-bounds write when calling clear_bit() in mm_cid_schedout(). The reviewer suggested adding a check for MM_CID_UNSET before setting the transition bit.
review_comments.raw_body:
The following commit has been merged into the core/urgent branch of tip:

Commit-ID:     de3ab9bd3133899efb92e4cd05ba4203e58fc0a3
Gitweb:        https://git.kernel.org/tip/de3ab9bd3133899efb92e4cd05ba4203e58fc0a3
Author:        Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>
AuthorDate:    Tue, 16 Jun 2026 16:38:17 -04:00
Committer:     Thomas Gleixner <tglx@kernel.org>
CommitterDate: Fri, 19 Jun 2026 21:44:16 +02:00

sched/mmcid: Fix OOB clear_bit when CID is MM_CID_UNSET in fixup path

In mm_cid_fixup_cpus_to_tasks(), when rq->curr has the target mm and
mm_cid.active is set, the CID is checked with cid_in_transit() before
setting the transition bit.  In per-CPU mode a newly forked or exec'd
task can be running with mm_cid.cid == MM_CID_UNSET because CIDs are
assigned lazily on schedule-in.  With cid_in_transit() the guard passes
for MM_CID_UNSET (no transit bit), converts it to MM_CID_UNSET |
MM_CID_TRANSIT and stores it back; later mm_cid_schedout() feeds this
to clear_bit() with MM_CID_UNSET as the bit number, triggering an
out-of-bounds write.

Symptoms: this is genuine memory corruption, but a bounded out-of-bounds
write, not an arbitrary one.  MM_CID_UNSET is the fixed sentinel BIT(31),
so once the bad value reaches mm_cid_schedout() the cid_from_transit_cid()
strip leaves MM_CID_UNSET, which fails the "cid < max_cids" convergence
test and falls into mm_drop_cid() -> clear_bit(MM_CID_UNSET,
mm_cidmask(mm)).  The cid bitmap is embedded in the mm_struct slab object
(after cpu_bitmap and mm_cpus_allowed) and is only num_possible_cpus()
bits wide, so clearing bit 31 is a deterministic OOB bit-clear at a
fixed offset of 2^31 / 8 == 256 MiB past the bitmap base.  The address is
not attacker-influenced (fixed sentinel -> fixed offset) and the op only
clears a single bit; what sits 256 MiB further along the direct map is
whatever kernel object happens to live there, so this corrupts one bit of
unpredictable kernel memory -- it is not an arbitrary-address or
arbitrary-value write.

It triggers only in per-CPU CID mode, when a CPU is running an active
task of the target mm whose cid is still MM_CID_UNSET -- the
fork()/execve() window before that task's next schedule-in assigns it a
real CID -- and a per-CPU -> per-task fixup walks over it (the mode
fallback driven by a thread exit, sched_mm_cid_exit(), or by the deferred
max_cids recompute in mm_cid_work_fn()).

In practice syzkaller surfaced it as a KASAN use-after-free reported in
__schedule -> mm_cid_switch_to, where the offending clear_bit() is inlined
via mm_cid_schedout() -> mm_drop_cid().

Guard the transition-bit assignment against MM_CID_UNSET, in addition to
the existing cid_in_transit() check, so the bit is only set on a genuine
task-owned CID.  A CPU-owned (MM_CID_ONCPU) CID of a running active task
is handled by the cid_on_cpu(pcp->cid) branch above and never reaches
this path, so excluding MM_CID_UNSET (and the already-transitioning case)
is sufficient.

Fixes: fbd0e71dc370 ("sched/mmcid: Provide CID ownership mode fixup functions")
Signed-off-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>
Signed-off-by: Thomas Gleixner <tglx@kernel.org>
Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8 syzkaller
Reviewed-by: Mathieu Desnoyers <mathieu.desnoyers@efficios.com>
Cc: stable@vger.kernel.org
Link: https://patch.msgid.link/20260616203818.1516263-1-riel@surriel.com
---
 kernel/sched/core.c | 15 +++++++++++++--
 1 file changed, 13 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)

diff --git a/kernel/sched/core.c b/kernel/sched/core.c
index 8b791e9..3cc6fb1 100644
--- a/kernel/sched/core.c
+++ b/kernel/sched/core.c
@@ -10909,8 +10909,19 @@ static void mm_cid_fixup_cpus_to_tasks(struct mm_struct *mm)
 		} else if (rq->curr->mm == mm && rq->curr->mm_cid.active) {
 			unsigned int cid = rq->curr->mm_cid.cid;
 
-			/* Ensure it has the transition bit set */
-			if (!cid_in_transit(cid)) {
+			/*
+			 * Set the transition bit only on a genuine task-owned
+			 * CID. A running active task can legitimately have
+			 * MM_CID_UNSET here: in per-CPU mode CIDs are assigned
+			 * lazily on schedule-in, so the fork()/execve() window
+			 * leaves the task active with no owned CID. Setting the
+			 * transition bit on MM_CID_UNSET would later feed
+			 * clear_bit() an out-of-bounds bit number via
+			 * mm_cid_schedout(), so exclude it. A CPU-owned
+			 * (MM_CID_ONCPU) CID is handled by the cid_on_cpu()
+			 * branch above and never reaches here.
+			 */
+			if (cid != MM_CID_UNSET && !cid_in_transit(cid)) {
 				cid = cid_to_transit_cid(cid);
 				rq->curr->mm_cid.cid = cid;
 				pcp->cid = cid;

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/3] x86/mm: use READ_ONCE/WRITE_ONCE for mm->context.untag_mask
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series modifies the x86/mm code to use READ_ONCE and WRITE_ONCE for accessing mm->context.untag_mask, a single aligned word that is written once when LAM is enabled and remains stable thereafter. The changes aim to make lockless access explicit and prevent potential tearing issues.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the patch without any specific comments or concerns.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 16 Jun 2026 15:02:58 -0400 Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com> wrote:

> mm->context.untag_mask is written once, when LAM is enabled
> (mm_enable_lam(), under mmap_write_lock and while the process is still
> single-threaded), and is otherwise stable and never reverted.
> untagged_addr_remote() reads it for a remote mm, and the new
> untagged_addr_remote_unlocked() (used by the per-VMA-lock
> access_remote_vm() fast path) reads it without the mmap lock.
> 
> The field is a single aligned word and cannot tear, but annotate the
> reads and writes with READ_ONCE()/WRITE_ONCE() to make the lockless
> access explicit and keep the compiler from reloading or tearing it.
> 
> No functional change.
> 
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> Signed-off-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>
> ---

>  arch/x86/include/asm/uaccess_64.h  | 2 +-
>  arch/x86/kernel/process_64.c       | 2 +-

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] mm/pagewalk: let folio_walk_start() run under the per-VMA lock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new flag FW_VMA_LOCKED for the folio_walk_start() function, allowing it to run under the per-VMA lock instead of the mmap lock. This is done to reduce contention and improve performance for callers that only need to read a single page. The patch also adds documentation explaining the behavior and requirements of this new flag.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: Raised concerns about the patch, including unclear explanations of why RCU-freed page tables are required and how higher-level page tables would be freed. Also questioned the need for CONFIG_MMU_GATHER_RCU_TABLE_FREE.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 16, 2026 at 03:02:59PM -0400, Rik van Riel wrote:
> folio_walk_start() asserts that the mmap lock is held.  For callers that
> only need to read a single, already-present page, the mmap lock is a
> heavy and often badly contended hammer: the VMA can instead be
> stabilized with the per-VMA lock, and the page table pages that are
> walked are kept alive by RCU page-table freeing
> (CONFIG_MMU_GATHER_RCU_TABLE_FREE).

See below, I don't think this is correct?

>
> Add an FW_VMA_LOCKED flag.  When passed, folio_walk_start() asserts the
> per-VMA lock instead of the mmap lock, requires RCU-freed page tables,
> and refuses hugetlb VMAs (PMD sharing cannot be walked safely this way).

This is mostly superfluous. You can just say you added the flag to use a VMA
flag. You put in parens the key thing about hugetlb, I think you should break
that out.

> Everything else folio_walk_start() relies on -- the page table locks,
> pmdp_get_lockless() and pte_offset_map_lock() -- is already safe without

is -> are.

> the mmap lock, mirroring the per-VMA lock page fault path.

I'm not sure I understand why you have to have RCU freed page tables but then
say that you didn't need it here? Strange to reference arbitrary functions from
folio_walk_start() too.

>
> No existing caller passes FW_VMA_LOCKED, so behaviour is unchanged.
>
> Assisted-by: Claude:claude-opus-4-8
> Signed-off-by: Rik van Riel <riel@surriel.com>
> ---
>  include/linux/pagewalk.h |  5 +++++
>  mm/pagewalk.c            | 18 ++++++++++++++++--
>  2 files changed, 21 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
>
> diff --git a/include/linux/pagewalk.h b/include/linux/pagewalk.h
> index b41d7265c01b..84dd0d68f747 100644
> --- a/include/linux/pagewalk.h
> +++ b/include/linux/pagewalk.h
> @@ -150,6 +150,11 @@ typedef int __bitwise folio_walk_flags_t;
>
>  /* Walk shared zeropages (small + huge) as well. */
>  #define FW_ZEROPAGE			((__force folio_walk_flags_t)BIT(0))
> +/*
> + * The caller holds the per-VMA lock instead of the mmap lock. Only valid with
> + * RCU-freed page tables (CONFIG_MMU_GATHER_RCU_TABLE_FREE) and not for hugetlb.
> + */

Hang on how could we be freeing higher level page tables of a VMA that's still
locked?

A VMA lock stabilises page tables for traversal, so why do you require
CONFIG_MMU_GATHER_RCU_TABLE_FREE here?

What will free the higher-level page tables?

Ref: https://origin.kernel.org/doc/html/latest/mm/process_addrs.html#page-table


> +#define FW_VMA_LOCKED			((__force folio_walk_flags_t)BIT(1))
>
>  enum folio_walk_level {
>  	FW_LEVEL_PTE,
> diff --git a/mm/pagewalk.c b/mm/pagewalk.c
> index 3ae2586ff45b..c85364b73e12 100644
> --- a/mm/pagewalk.c
> +++ b/mm/pagewalk.c
> @@ -890,7 +890,9 @@ int walk_page_mapping(struct address_space *mapping, pgoff_t first_index,
>   * huge_ptep_set_*, ...). Note that the page table entry stored in @fw might
>   * not correspond to the first physical entry of a logical hugetlb entry.
>   *
> - * The mmap lock must be held in read mode.
> + * The mmap lock must be held in read mode. Alternatively, if @FW_VMA_LOCKED is
> + * passed, the VMA's per-VMA lock must be held (only supported with RCU-freed
> + * page tables, i.e. CONFIG_MMU_GATHER_RCU_TABLE_FREE, and not for hugetlb).
>   *
>   * Return: folio pointer on success, otherwise NULL.

> @@ -908,7 +910,19 @@ struct folio *folio_walk_start(struct folio_walk *fw,
>  	pgd_t *pgdp;

>
> -	mmap_assert_locked(vma->vm_mm);
> +	if (flags & FW_VMA_LOCKED) {

Err, but we take locks as normal on the walk?

> +		 * Hugetlb (PMD sharing) is not supported on this path.

I don't get the explanation above and then you just write a line that says what

You should explain why hugetlb isn't supported.
